there does not appear to be a simpler solution at the moment, as noted [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/35064646/how-to-access-a-module-installed-by-setuptools-install-requires-within-setup-py#35116358) and discussed [here](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/distutils-sig/2015-March/025882.html). the extension modules require eigency to locate paths during setup, and including eigency as a dependency under `install_requires` (the equivalent of requirements.txt for setuptools) or even `setup_requires` does not fix the issue. there may be a way to subclass the import in setup.py that i am not sure how to do, which i have opened as an issue on the eigency [page](https://github.com/wouterboomsma/eigency/issues/16). 

therefore adding one line to the install process, `pip install eigency`, is the most straightforward solution at the moment. you are welcome to submit a pull request if you have a better option.

Just an update on this - as I came up with a way to pass ANY object thought akka. Here is how you can patch the ThreadingActor. Of course, you would inherit from this class instead for creating all your actors:

```
from pykka import ThreadingActor, ActorRef


class Actor(ThreadingActor):
    """
    Actor class from which classes should inherit to create an actor.

    Adapter to the pykka actors that treats messages as instances
    of some class. If pykka's system message is encountered,
    this adapter simply passes the message on.
    """
    PAYLOAD_MARKER = '__actor.message.payload__'

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(Actor, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        # Override actor ref with our custom actor ref,
        # that creates a dict each time it sends a message
        self.actor_ref = ActorRefAdaptor(self)

    def _handle_receive(self, message_or_dict):
        if Actor.PAYLOAD_MARKER in message_or_dict:
            # This is our message with an instance of the object in it - pass it
            # straight to on receive function
            self.on_receive(message_or_dict[Actor.PAYLOAD_MARKER])
        else:
            # hand the message over to pykka
            super(Actor, self)._handle_receive(message_or_dict)


class ActorRefAdaptor(ActorRef):
    def tell(self, message):
        # If this is a dict message, it might be pykka's system message,
        # and we leave it as is.
        if isinstance(message, dict):
            super(ActorRefAdaptor, self).tell(message)
        else:
            # Our message is an instance of some class, but we need
            #to pass pykka a dict object - so we wrap it in one!
            super(ActorRefAdaptor, self).tell({Actor.PAYLOAD_MARKER: message})
```

I just tried the above script with similar image sizes and keypoints as in your example. Still seems to generate reasonable outputs (see below). Do you maybe happen to resize the images before or after the augmentation? It is possible that you are working with the wrong image shape in your keypoints, leading to them being rotated on a larger image than the one that you augment. In the below script, you can uncomment `#kps = [ia.KeypointsOnImage(kps, shape=(img.shape[0]+50, img.shape[1]+50))]` (and comment the other `kps=...` line) to see the effect.

```python
from __future__ import print_function, division
import imgaug as ia
from imgaug import augmenters as iaa
from scipy import misc, ndimage
import numpy as np

ia.seed(57)

def drawpoint(img, kp, size=4):
    h, w = img.shape[0:2]
    x = kp.x
    y = kp.y
    x1 = x - size
    x2 = x
    y1 = y - size
    y2 = y
    if 0 <= x1 < w and 0 <= x2 < w and 0 <= y1 < h and 0 <= y2 < h:
        img[y1:y2, x1:x2, 0] = 255
    return img

def drawpoints(img, kps, size=4):
    for kp in kps:
        drawpoint(img, kp, size=size)
    return img

sw = 256  # size of the image
sh = 305  # size of the image
pd = 25  # positions of each corner pixel (e.g. at (25, 25), (-25, 25), ...)
ps = 6  # size of each square to draw
img = np.zeros((sh, sw, 3), dtype=np.uint8)  # black example image
# add white squares in the corners
img[pd - ps:pd, pd - ps:pd, :] = 255
img[-pd - ps:-pd, pd - ps:pd, :] = 128
img[pd - ps:pd, -pd - ps:-pd, :] = 64
img[-pd - ps:-pd, -pd - ps:-pd, :] = 255
img[0, :, :] = 20
img[-1, :, :] = 20
img[:, 0, :] = 20
img[:, -1, :] = 20

# define one test keypoint at (100, 100)
kps_coords = [(50, 75), (150, 75), (100, 120), (60, 160), (120, 160)]
kps = []
img_with_kp = np.copy(img)
for kp_x, kp_y in kps_coords:
    img_with_kp[kp_y - ps:kp_y, kp_x - ps:kp_x, 2] = 255
    kps.append(ia.Keypoint(kp_x, kp_y))
kps = [ia.KeypointsOnImage(kps, shape=img.shape)]
#kps = [ia.KeypointsOnImage(kps, shape=(img.shape[0]+50, img.shape[1]+50))]
img_org2 = kps[0].draw_on_image(np.copy(img_with_kp))

nb_augs = 1000  # how often to augment here
aug_seq = iaa.Sequential({
    iaa.Affine(rotate=(-60, 60), shear=(-20, 20))  # rotate -40 to 40 deg
})
aug_aff = iaa.Affine(rotate=(-60, 60), shear=(-20, 20))

###############
# make deterministic
###############
aug_seq = aug_seq.to_deterministic()
aug_aff = aug_aff.to_deterministic()
###############

# augment images
imgs_seq_aug = aug_seq.augment_image(img_with_kp)
imgs_aff_aug = aug_aff.augment_image(img_with_kp)

# augment keypoints
kps_seq_aug = []
kps_aff_aug = []
kps_seq_aug = aug_seq.augment_keypoints(kps)
kps_aff_aug = aug_aff.augment_keypoints(kps)
#seq_x, seq_y = kps_seq_aug[0].keypoints[0].x, kps_seq_aug[0].keypoints[0].y
#aff_x, aff_y = kps_aff_aug[0].keypoints[0].x, kps_aff_aug[0].keypoints[0].y

# code to test draw_on_image
img_org = kps[0].draw_on_image(np.copy(img_with_kp))
img_seq_aug = kps_seq_aug[0].draw_on_image(np.copy(imgs_seq_aug))
img_aff_aug = kps_aff_aug[0].draw_on_image(np.copy(imgs_aff_aug))
img_seq_aug2 = drawpoints(np.copy(img_seq_aug), kps_seq_aug[0].keypoints)
img_aff_aug2 = drawpoints(np.copy(img_aff_aug), kps_aff_aug[0].keypoints)
# misc.imshow(np.hstack([img_seq_aug, img_aff_aug, img_org.astype(np.uint8)]))

# plot
misc.imsave("test_image2.png", np.hstack([img_seq_aug2, img_aff_aug2, img_org]))

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hlDhp-eNLMU

It's 41 secs long, no sound, with annotations. Used chrome but firefox is pretty much the same thing.

The programmatic_auth url:

https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/programmatic_auth?hl=en&scope=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.google.com%2Faccounts%2FOAuthLogin+https%3A%2F%2Fwww.googleapis.com%2Fauth%2Fuserinfo.email&client_id=936475272427.apps.googleusercontent.com&access_type=offline&delegated_client_id=183697946088-m3jnlsqshjhh5lbvg05k46q1k4qqtrgn.apps.googleusercontent.com&top_level_cookie=1

Text version:

1. Go to that url
2. Enter your username, click next.
3. Right click page background, inspect
4. Go to the network tab.
5. Enter your password, click sign in
6. Click the first row, the one that says "programmatic_auth"
7. Scroll down in the right-side panel, find "set-cookie"
8. Your code should be there, after "oauth_code=", up to but not including the semicolon.
9. Copy it and use it.

and to confirm we can use the command:
output = net_connect.send_command("show privilege")
print(output)

Actually, my patch retains the partition information (still more like `iostat -p`). The problem with #1312 is that it always ignores parent devices if they have partitions.

So, this PR always strips partition information, and #1312 always strips parent devices, regardless of `perdisk`.

Indeed, the best thing to do is probably return the partition information and _if_ `perdisk` is False then skip devices that have partitions. This is pretty easy with #1312, but for that we need to pass `perdisk` to the platform modules - all `disk_io_counters` will have to get the `perdisk` parameter. 

If we don't want to do that then it's your call - #1312 will return more information (more like today, except without the wrong multiple counting), and this PR will always strip partitions (but this PR is far simpler in terms of code).

In addition, I think divide the cost to the square of an external input variance is useless since the value of variance is a constant and does not effect the cost.

For me, the solution was this:
`sudo vim /etc/php-fpm.d/mywebsite.conf`

Find the line: `env[PATH] = /usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin` and uncomment it.

Then restart php-fpm and it should be working. You might also need a service file as well with something very similar content:

```
[Unit]
Description=Unoconv listener for document conversions
Documentation=https://github.com/dagwieers/unoconv
After=network.target remote-fs.target nss-lookup.target

[Service]
Type=simple
Environment="UNO_PATH=/usr/lib64/libreoffice/program"
ExecStart=/usr/bin/unoconv --listener

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```
The think you might need to modify is the UNO_PATH which is the path for your libreoffice installation. Hope I've helped. (I'm on CentOS, unable to tell you exactly the file routes)

Here we have an API-based usage of coverage. By passing `rcfile` instead of `coverage.config` the code (https://github.com/nedbat/coveragepy/blob/master/coverage/multiproc.py#L34) mandates the use of configuration file that is simply not available when coverage is used from the API. 

The example of such usage is given here: 
https://github.com/pybuilder/pybuilder/pull/384/files#diff-62a96813032655a36d7cf13b53e2cea5R107

One of the versions of the fix are given here: https://github.com/pybuilder/pybuilder/pull/384/files#diff-df782129901fdd05d66cf8a732aa363bR58

Observe, that instead of `rcfile` the `Stowaway` object passes `coverage.config` and reinitializes `Coverage` with it in a subprocess.

You should be able to avoid this situation by ensuring that the MySQL `wait_timeout` and sqlservice `SQL_POOL_RECYCLE` values are compatible. Basically, you need `SQL_POOL_RECYCLE` to be less than `wait_timeout`.

For example, if you had the following `my.cnf`:

```ini
[mysqld]
wait_timeout = 600
```

Then, you'd want `SQL_POOL_RECYCLE` to be less than 600 (e.g. 300).

```python
from sqlservice import SQLClient

db = SQLClient({'SQL_POOL_RECYCLE': 300, ...}, ...)
```

That way SQLAlchemy will recycle the connection before MySQL closes it due to exceeding its own timeout.

But if you still get error and want to reconnect, you can just call `db.remove()` to remove the session and obtain a new connection.

There's also "pessimistic disconnect handling" which can also be used to pre-ping a connection before it's checked out from the pool to ensure it is usable: http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/pooling.html#disconnect-handling-pessimistic

Sorry, but you have the concepts wrong. When you see a *day* bar is because the bar is **complete** and the day is **over**.

  - Monday: you get *daily* bar (market is closed) and you calculate something based on the "daily" already closed bar. You get a signal. If you issue an order at this point in time, the *opening* price of the next day is unknown, because the day has not yet started.

  - Tuesday: when you see the *daily* bar, just like the bar on Monday, the bar is closed, the day is over. You can no longer buy at the *open* price (unless it happens to repeat at a later point in time)

Your broker gives you the *opening* price in real-time, i.e. intraday. If the broker is so nice as to make a promise with regards to this price and guarantee execution for you it must be a very nice broker. The brokers in this part of the world make no execution guarantee on a price which has already happened. The *opening* price may be the highest price of the day and no longer seen in the entire session (or the lowest)

If you want to do something before Tuesday closes, and especially near to the opening moment you have to do it with intraday prices, because you operate on a non-yet closed daily bar. The daily bar does not exist yet because the day is not yet over.

1) Feel free to use `self.data.close[1]`. You can also use `self.data.close[1000]`. It is exactly so pointless as `1`.

2) Intraday is of course supported, there are several examples of it and it includes *resampling* and *replaying* of intraday data.

3) Use the proper timeframe (intraday) and not the wrong timeframe *day* for the order execution.

> -  estimating standard deviation 2 *
>     a small thing indeed but still there: while estimating the standard deviation you should not divide by N but by N-1 (why not use built-in functions anyway?)

This would apply (Bessel's correction) if the *returns* were a sample of the population. But in this case the population is complete. Two years and 2 returns.

> (why not use built-in functions anyway?)

You may be referring to the *statistics* module that as far as I know is available first with Python `3.4`. Unfortunately the way chosen by *Python* to move people away from `2.7` is to not make things available. Hence the need for home-cooked equivalents.

> I am unclear how you arrive at the annual returns you state from the quick start cross-over example (since mine, calculated manually, being 14.89% (2005) and 5.05% (2006) but I might have made mistakes in the SMA cross-over)

The `SharpeRatio` analyzer internally uses the `TimeReturn` analyzer Those values are the ones returned by the `TimeReturn` analyzer. (in the next comment there is a modified sample which has an independent `TimeReturn` and prints the values of the analyzer and the actual portfolio value each day.

  - 2005 Start: 10000.00 End: 10232.86 -> Return: 0.023286
  - 2006 Start: 10232.86 End: 10496.68 -> Return: 0.025782

Which are the same values returned by the `TimeReturn` analyzer

> Estimating standard deviation *
> from only two annual values like it is done in this example has two hefty caveats:
> Firstly, results will be statistically extremely weak, ideally you should use 30 or more data points. Secondly, by compressing the time frame so radically before the actual calculation, you lose all the inter-year information about the actual movements of the asset, basically wiping out any of the up-and-then-down-moves (which very exactly leaves you with such small values for sd). Why throw all this information away?

The documentation shows how to simply apply the `SharpeRatio` which is configured by default to use `TimeFrame.Years` as the default and the data sample only has 2 years. The documentation is not seeking the best possible `SharpeRatio`. It is obvious that a large sample is needed for a better statistic and evaluation.

No information is lost: the sample is explicitly requesting yearly returns. You can also tell the `SharpeRatio` to use `TimeFrame.Daily` and it will then consider then daily returns for the calculation.

> Better calculate std dev from monthly or daily returns and then annualize it. If they are normally distributed, we can do so by multiplying with the square root of the time scaling factor (there are 252 trading days in one year: -> ann. Std. dev. = daily std dev. * sqrt(252) )

The code in `SharpeRatio` tries already this if you tell `convertrate=False`. In this case the *returns* will be annualized, instead of downgrading the ratio to the given timeframe. This is possibly not the best naming convention.

All other points seem to refer to a *modernized* version of the `SharpeRatio` and not to the original definition which uses the arithmetic mean.

This doesn't grant modifying the `SharpeRatio` itself. A `SharpeRatioModern` can be added to avoid false expectations about what the `SharpeRatio` is returning.

Got it to work :D

From at least Django 1.7+, the `{% include %}` [accepts](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.7/django/template/loader_tags.py#L139) a "template-like" having a ``.render()``. So, it is easier to just ``.get_template()`` on a filter and use this one when including:

```python
# myapp/templatetags/get_template.py
from django import template
from django.template import loader

register = template.Library()

@register.filter
def get_template(name):
    return loader.get_template(name)   # <- Uses default template "search" for a engine
```

```django
{# myapp/templates/djangotemplate.html #}
{% load get_template %}
{% include 'myapp/template.jinja'|get_template %}
```

It works up to Django 1.10.X.

On Django 1.11, however, [its internals](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.11/django/template/loader_tags.py#L207) does some trick searching for ``template.template`` and that breaks django-jinja.

There is a fix on PR #201 to make it work on Django 1.11.

And covariance matrix

With arrays of tuples:

These objects could still be iterated through faster than in the case of a sparse array. The tradeoff is really checking if a given key is in a given list. In the F matrix calculation, when summing up values for each element, whether a source pixel mapped to an image pixel could be determined in constant time. Now that operation would take take the average number of image pixels per source pixel to perform.
There are possibly other downsides to dictionaries. A hash table (i.e. the data structure that a dictionary uses) stores values in an array with the index given by hashing the key. The hash of an integer value is just that integer value. If we had two differently labelled image pixels (let’s say 2 and 42) then the dictionary would be allocating a larger array than necessary.

> On 6 Feb 2018, at 23:28, Jammy2211 <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> 
> Thinking about it, the code pretty much falls into two regimes:
> 
> Image-plane - here the data is well structured and we have prior knowledge of how the best store data. For example, we know how many image pixels are in a mask, how my sub pixels are on the image pixel grid and how many deflection angles / light intensities we'll need to compute. This lends itself straight forwardly to NumPy arrays, which can be optimized via Cython.
> 
> Source plane - here, all hell breaks loose. Our source plane matrices are sparse (f, F, H), and due to the random nature of the traced image coordinates and clustering we have no knowledge of how a given matrix should be set up in 1D. Thus, we have opted for a dictionary representation, which does not rely on predefined array sizes so as to exploit sparsity.
> 
> Linking the image-plane NumPy arrays to the source plane dictionaries is straightforward. My concern is the dictionary representation (which handles the most intensive routines) may prove difficult to optimize, as it cannot be directly optimized via Cython. Sure, it completely circumvents sparseness issues, but it may just be slower in pure processing terms than NumPy arrays with Cython
> 
> Is there an efficient way to represent f, F and H using NumPy arrays which is amenable to Cython, Or do these arrays require copious amounts of size tracking / indexing to run?
> 
> If we used 2D numpy arrays for f, F and H, can we efficiently track where the non-zero terms are to overcome sparseness? Certainly, we have what we need here for f and H already, and our contiguous source-plane search for F might provide what we need there too.
> 
> (For now, we'll get the code up with NumPy in image plane, dicts in source-plane, then back to Numpy for the inversion, and profile it. Just tracking what the best optimization strategy might be - albeit its hopeless without profiling!)

It looks like this error can be caused by many things including [improper DNS forwarding](https://askubuntu.com/questions/91543/apt-get-update-fails-to-fetch-files-temporary-failure-resolving-error) or a [misconfigured DHCP server on your network](https://serverfault.com/questions/266042/temporary-failure-in-name-resolution/329009#329009).  Depending on the cause, the problem probably won't just go away.  If you run the script with these DNS errors, and the list of mirrors is long, it would needlessly try to resolve each mirror name before reporting nothing, and there would likely be later [problems](https://answers.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/apt/+question/197664) updating or installing using `apt-get`.

That said, I agree that error handling shouldn't ignore unlikely scenarios, so exiting may not be the best solution for this or something like an outdated or dead mirror address even though it's not likely that Canonical would provide a bad address in `mirrors.txt`.

That's the error we want to prevent by implementing Voluptuous. We want to be able to check that, if given a file name, the destination/target directory it is located in exists. So if I'm given `'/tmp/foo.log'`, I want to check that `/tmp` is a valid directory. I can't pass this string (`'/tmp/foo.log'`) directly to `IsDir` because `foo.log` isn't a directory. I can't always use `IsFile` because if `foo.log` hasn't been created yet, it raises an exception.

For now I've decided to just use a lambda function to cut off the file name from the string and pass the result to `IsDir`, which works fine.

Hi, @futetest. The [ClosestMeaningAdapter](https://github.com/gunthercox/ChatterBot/blob/master/chatterbot/adapters/logic/closest_meaning.py) compare all the words.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2030578/17620135/2ce1b046-6058-11e6-809b-31c6f89933fa.png)

To explain a bit further, it selects the highest value combination of matching tokenized versions of the words in each pair being compared. To do this, it first creates a list of the tokenized words for both statements being compared using NLTK's `get_tokens` function. It then produces what is essentially a matrix of each possible combination of the ordering of the tokens in both lists using Pythons built in `itertools.product` function.

> Note, that the reason that the order of the words is ignored is because the goal of this adapter is to compare the meaning of each word, so order was deemed an invalid heuristic.

The adapter selects and returns the statement with the highest matching token list comparison to the input statements.

@micjoc00, your device HID descriptor has non consistent usage ids, the Windows HID descriptor parser is kind of picky about value arrays, and looks like the Silicon Labs took a shortcut trying to simplify the descriptor.

Anyway... by default, `.get()` tries to parse the retrieved device data using the device supplied HID descriptors.

Actually here, everything is usage id '1', even for some items that are not value arrays, like you mentioned as in feature report 3 (report_count=1), which doesn't even follow the AN495 specification that states that there are actually 2 bytes on it, is likely windows matches the wrong capabilities due the id/page match but different report count field: result, the usage in report 3 is not a value array as reported in report 5.

If this is about something you can provide technical feedback, try suggesting using consistent (separate values, specially dedicated ones for different fields not actually only for full reports) usage IDs.

This would avoid the "byte and offset" specification at all, the HID descriptor would provide the right schema for how to access and find specific fields. This is the purpose of having standard usage pages, and that's why your USB HID button controls for volume just work, regardless of the report id a device would use or the actual byte and bit offset for button toggles.

Anyway, just use raw reports to avoid the broken device descriptor Windows can't cope with.

Try calling `report.get(do_process_raw_report = False)` or just `report.get(False)`, this will skip any attempt to decode the usages based on the device HID report.

@rytilahti I've decided to try and port the library to `asyncio`. I can say that I already have a working communication based on `asyncio`'s `DatagramProtocol`. I've implemented two `DatagramProtocol` classes: `MIIOProtocol` and `MIIODiscoverProtocol`.

The `MIIODiscoverProtocol` implements the handshake/discovery logic. Like in the current implementation, it can work either with broadcast and wait for multiple responses (it stores the results in `asyncio.Queue` so external `asyncio` code can consume the results on the fly, but also a callback callable can be provided which is called on each response) or with a single host and returns the response right away.

The `MIIOProtocol` implements the request/response logic with timeouts and retries. It supports parallel requests (for example I can send two get_prop requests without waiting for first one to return before requesting the other set of properties). This class also uses the `MIIODiscoveryProtocol` to do the handshake, but I've implemented it so that it doesn't do handshake before every request, but works in a loop with some sleep time (I'm using 60 seconds right now). I'm using an `asyncio.Event` instance to make sure that the request can't be made before first successful handshake and then during every next scheduled one (the send coroutine waits on the `Event` object to be set).

I haven't modified any other code or class in the library yet. I've been able to successfully reuse the construct schema. But I didn't touch any devices' code yet. I'm testing directly on my new classes.
Also something needs to be done with the `zeroconf` library. The one currently in use doesn't support `asyncio`. I've found `aiozeroconf` which is a direct port of the `zeroconf` for `asyncio`.

When I'm happy with the code I'll push this as a branch to my fork, but I probably won't create a PR right away. I'll post it here so it can be decided what's next.

Sounds reasonable to me, I'd accept a patch. I don't know how hard it is to do it right, though.. in Tahoe we did something similar, and it works horrendously differently on windows (see [allmydata.util.fileutil.get_disk_stats](https://github.com/tahoe-lafs/tahoe-lafs/blob/master/src/allmydata/util/fileutil.py#L443)). Alternatively we could just do `open(); seek(SIZE); write("\x00"); seek(0)`, but if the platform supports sparse files, that won't actually fail. We could do `write("\x00" * size)` (or a less memory-hungry equivalent), but that'd introduce a significant delay for large files. I can imagine having some highly asynchronous receiver running a loop that tries to populate the whole file (with zeros), and then when each new record arrives over the wire, it seeks back to the right place and writes the record, then seeks back to the end and keeps appending: that would avoid the delay, and would still fail earlier than before, but would still fail "late" sometimes, and the state machine would be kinda heinous.

The deeper problem is that we can't know the size of the incoming file until we've consumed the code, so the sender will always have to start again (and the receiver will always have to ask the sender to start again, which is a big crimp in the workflow). I have some ideas in mind for resumable transfers, but they'll depend upon using the same sending and receiving machines (it'll store a full-sized key in some small file on both of them).

Reopening to point out how attrs does factory functions. It's basically my original 2: `field(default=Factory(callable), ...)`. The `attr.Factory` class has an additional optional parameter: `takes_self` http://www.attrs.org/en/stable/api.html#attr.Factory. The docstring says: `Pass the partially initialized instance that is being initialized as a positional argument`. That seems like a useful feature, and allows a factory function for a field to depend on earlier defined fields.

But requiring the separate `Factory()` seems like a lot to pay for a feature that would be used only infrequently. We could do something horrible like inspect the callable, and if it takes one parameter, pass self. See below for attrs discussions of such magic.

I'm only bringing this up for completeness. As with most of these issues, the attrs team has put a lot of good thought in to it.

See https://github.com/python-attrs/attrs/pull/178 and https://github.com/python-attrs/attrs/issues/189.

Yes, I thought you would ask that, so in order to explain, let me introduce my usecase (I was never the theoretical guy:) ):

I have an application, for which I plan to have more skins, that is templates, css (SASS) files, javascript files. So I have the following directory structure for the static files:

```
static/
-- bower_components/
-- skins/
---- app.build.js
---- default/
------ scss/
------ js/
-------- common.js
-------- common.build.js
-------- sidebar.js and other JS modules for the skin
---- dark/
------ scss/
------ js/
-------- common.js
-------- common.build.js
-------- other JS modules for the skin
```

So as you see, I use the `common.js` for each skin as an entry point, and then load the widget modules for the respective widgets inlined (so I can pass them some dynamic configuration).

I have the following config for `django-require`:
```python
REQUIRE_BASE_URL = 'skins'
REQUIRE_JS = '../bower_components/requirejs/require.js'
REQUIRE_BUILD_PROFILE = 'app.build.js'
REQUIRE_DEBUG = DEBUG
REQUIRE_STANDALONE_MODULES = {
    'default/js/common': {
        'out': 'default/js/common-built.js',
        'build_profile': 'default/js/common.build.js',
    }
}
```

which works in development (`DEBUG = True`) mode, with using `{% require_module 'default/js/common' %}`, but fails when I try to build the modules. The reason is the wrong baseUrl specification. While using it in development mode, the tag produces the following output:
```html
<script src="/static/bower_components/requirejs/require.js" data-main="/static/skins/default/js/common.js"></script>
```

Which means, RequireJS will set its `baseUrl` to the module path, so everything would work just fine when importing the sidebar module, for example:
```html
<script type="text/javascript">
    require(['sidebar'], function (sidebar) {
      sidebar.init({
        selectors: {
            buttonToggle: '.navbar-toggle',
            sidebarWrapper: '.sidebar-wrapper',
            sidebarOverlay: '.sidebar-offcanvas-overlay'
        }
      });
    });
</script>
```
But fails to build when doing `collectstatic`, as the `baseUrl` setting is overridden there: adding `sidebar.js` to the included modules in `common.build.js` will result the compilation failing because of searching for that file under `skins/`.

What I'd like to achieve is to have each compiled module placed under `/static/skins/<skinname>/js/`, that's where the relative` BASE_URL` comes in. So I started tweaking your code, adding the relative `BASE_URL`, and came to the conclusion that `almond.js` has to be copied in the same directory level. That's okay, but another problem occurs: since I use `'default/js/common'` as the modules name, which has a path in it in order to use it in the HTML properly, the introduced relative path will break the filename. So here I'd like to introduce a filename next to the relative path, while using the config key only as a property key, rather than a something that refers to a filename.

---------

The second thing I'd like to achieve is to make sure that `common.js` (which contains the require.config AND the basic module definition) gets loaded before all modules in development mode. RequireJS advises to use two `<script>` tags in the header part for that, one for loading `require.js` itself, and then the other to load `common.js`. This would load these two scripts before everything else, since they would be in the header at the top level. The point of this is, this way I won't need to require `common.js` in each inline require tag, as suggested [here](http://requirejs.org/docs/api.html#data-main).

So in development mode, my new tag would produce a tag something like this:
```html
<script src="/static/bower_components/requirejs/require.js"></script>
<script src="/static/skins/default/js/common.js"></script>
```
Of course for this approach, I'd have to set `baseUrl` in the config, but that's something I can live with. Since at `collectstatic` I'd compile everything together in one file with almond, this wouldn't be an issue there.

------------------

To sum up:
- Adding entry point filenames and relative paths to the currently working tag would break the existing functionality for most of the people
- The new tag would produce a slightly different HTML output in development mode, for which a different approach is needed.

I'm really open for a discussion on this, but keep in mind I've been playing around with this in the last several days trying to find a way, and to me this seems the only plausible solution.

Most in-memory caches, including Redis, can and are expected to run in an mode that will automatically evict old (LRU, LFU) items as space runs out. It's not a memory leak so much as an intentional behavior.

It might be worth adding a note to the docs that if you're using a Redis cache you'll need to examine your eviction policies—which are several settings that interact, including `maxmemory` and `maxmemory-policy`—configured appropriately.

I like the idea of `ratelimit.contrib.drf` (or `ratelimit.ext.drf` or similar) but I think it'd be better to keep code for DRF isolated there. Most of the `@ratelimit` decorator is decorator boilerplate for functools, so it wouldn't be a hard thing to copy it to `@ratelimit.ext.drf.ratelimit` and have it:

1. have the same API as `ratelimit.decorators.ratelimit`,
2. share the same counters,
3. look for DRF's `Request` object instead of Django's `HttpRequest` (or both) and
4. have it throw `Throttled` instead of `Ratelimited`.

Whether or not that's exactly what we should do, I increasingly thing a separate module to support other DRF-like modules is the right way to go.
Never mind, it can be done by callable key (although it doesn't work with 0.5 due to missing `request` argument).

the displayed collection is somehow an internal variable for your view to access the current displayed collection (sorted,filtered,paginated) whereas the safe source is the whole dataset loaded. Sometimes they are exactly the same (when you put all the logic (sort, filter,etc) in the client and therefore you have loaded all the data, or when you put all the logic in the server and the response from the server matches exactly the table state -see pipe/ajax plugin section-). Sometimes they are not, like if you want to delete or remove rows from you dataset, you have to tell smart-table to watch the reference collection by using st-safe-src (most of the time if you load  your data after the table has been rendered and initialized)
Yes indeed it adds a property to your model when you select a row and this might be a bit intrusive, but note that the items within both collection (displayed and reference) are the same references, so if you add a property to an item in a collection, this property will be added to the same item in the other. 
However displayedCollection.indexOf(item) may be different than safeSourceCollection.indexOf(item) for the same item and displayedCollection.indexOf(item) may change for the same item whenever the tableState change

A `Table` object in sqlalchemy can reference either a table or a view. So there should be no syntactic difference between querying a view vs. a table.

The only reason that this package is necessary is that `CREATE VIEW` and `DROP VIEW` actually require different syntax. In a `SELECT` statement, there's nothing to distinguish how you query a table vs. a view, so sqlalchemy doesn't need to know if the relation is a table or an object in that case.

I see what is happening. I'm testing and developing two different Flask applications: 
- The one that was failing is a native Flask application with Flask-Login and the request_loader.
- The other one is just a prove of concepts: a test to check whether [Flask-AppBuilder](http://flask-appbuilder.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) could help. Flask-AppBuilder also uses Flask-Login and right now it is configured to use login through `AUTH_DB`.

I run both apps from the same host, though they use different ports. I just wanted to check Flask-AppBuilder as a way to save a full development from scratch but both apps shared their host and many config params. Since both apps are being served from the same host and accessed through localhost, I didn't realize that both apps were sharing the same `session` cookie. Furthermore, I didn't changed the `SECRET_KEY`. Therefore both apps understood, could read and could write the same session cookie. 

Firstly, I have changed the `SECRET_KEY`of one of those apps: 
Once the `SECRET_KEY` is changed the cookie contents are not shared anymore between apps, but the cookie name `session` and its site `localhost` are. Now when any of both applications tries to read the `session` cookie, it only succeeds if the cookie has been signed by the application itself and not by the other one. Otherwise  the application replaces the cookie with an own one.

Secondly, I have set a different `SESSION_COOKIE_NAME` for each app. This actually has solved the problem: Cookies with different names are generated for each app.

Now it is clear to me that I have messed up two different sessions and that was causing the unexpected behaviour. (*ashamed*)

This is basically the same issue as #30.
The problem is that there is no unique layout algorithm (because the venn diagram lacks one degree of freedom). The current one is focused on representing pairwise intersections faithfully, at the price of misrepresenting triple-intersections at times.

I will probably add a different layout algorithm eventually, but so far your best bet is to manually tune the sizes using `venn3_unweighted` (see the referenced issue for an example).

I am totally waiting for constructive suggestions regarding the alternative layout algorithm btw.

When they create an access or refresh token using the username and password supplied, you can store the created token in a database with their username and a revoked status of `unrevoked`. Then, when you want to actually revoke the refresh tokens, you can go to the database, find tokens that have been created by the given username, and update the revoked status in the database. Basically you are storing the tokens at creation time, instead of at revocation time.

Here is an example you could use to get started with this: https://github.com/vimalloc/flask-jwt-extended/tree/master/examples/database_blacklist

Hello,

*hvad* does not have the concept of a default language. You can either:

 - use untranslated versions of your models, using `.untranslated()`.
 - or specify a language to load, using `.language('en')`.

In the latter case, you may omit the language (`.language()`), which *hvad* interprets as *“ask Django what the currently active language is and use that”*. If your Django is configured correctly, that language should be that of the request being handled.

Under the hood, *hvad* is very different from the package you linked. *hvad* uses a separate table for translated fields.

Hope it helps, feel free to ask if you need more details.

#### pyinstaller
the pyinstaller `latency.spec` file (put next to `latency.py`):
```python
# -*- mode: python -*-

result = Analysis(
  ['latency.py'],
  hiddenimports=['engineio.async_eventlet'],
  hookspath=None,
  runtime_hooks=None
  )

# from pyinstaller wiki
def Datafiles(*filenames, **kw):
    import os

    def datafile(path, strip_path=True):
        parts = path.split('/')
        path = name = os.path.join(*parts)
        if strip_path:
            name = os.path.basename(path)

        return name, path, 'DATA'

    strip_path = kw.get('strip_path', True)
    return TOC(
        datafile(filename, strip_path=strip_path)
        for filename in filenames
        if os.path.isfile(filename)
        )

templates = Datafiles('templates/latency.html', strip_path=False)
static = Datafiles('static/style.css', strip_path=False)

pyz = PYZ(result.pure)

exe = EXE(
  pyz,
  result.scripts,
  result.binaries,
  result.zipfiles,
  result.datas,
  templates,
  static,
  name='latency.exe',
  debug=False,
  strip=None,
  upx=True,
  console=True
  )
```

#### conclusion

That's expected if you look at how MapQuery is implemented: https://github.com/mvexel/overpass-api-python-wrapper/blob/master/overpass/queries.py#L7

MapQuery first gets all nodes in the bbox, and then finds associated ways/relations. As ways are not cut in the same way on the bbox, some nodes are missing. 

You can see the same effect here: http://overpass-turbo.eu/s/kCE

Worse, the query is just the opposite, instead of deleting expired uploads, it deletes non expired uploads:

    -        qs = qs.filter(created_on__gte=(timezone.now() - EXPIRATION_DELTA))
    +        qs = qs.filter(created_on__lt=(timezone.now() - EXPIRATION_DELTA))

As far as I know executing `python` (at least on unix-based os) spawns a new process that has a copy of the environment variables of the parent process but it cannot modify the variables of the parent process. So as far as I can see there is no possibility to change the PATH in the calling proccess.

I think there are two workarounds that might resolve this issue:

- Adding a Python function that appends an `export PATH=$PATH:...` to the users ~/.profile file so it is always set when starting a new shell. 
- Installing a small bash script during `pip install` that exports the PATH. This script then needs to be run with `source name-of-script` or `. name-of-script` to be able to set environment variables of the calling process

In my opinion the first option is not very appropiate because it easily messes up your .profile (e.g. it will not clean up the .profile file on `pip uninstall`). As far as I'm concerned the second option is the best way of setting the PATH variable outside the Python process.

Something to keep in mind on this too is Facebook's flow is a bit wonky for their code flow. If you have native apps utilizing their SDK (which more than likely is what this project targets), they will be handing an access token to you (no way to get a code using their SDK, which is the best user experience and recommended). It's what Facebook calls their "long-lived" token, which means it's good for 60 days. If you want to get ahold of the expires time for this, you need to then take that access token, make their code call with that access token, which will give you a code to then use to make another call to get the access token with expires, you can then store this server side and know reliably when you'll need to refresh that access token before it expires... once it expires you'll need to have the user do the auth flow again, which isn't ideal. To get around that, and I realize this part is outside the scope of this project, you'll need a background process/job (ie cron, etc) that checks for tokens that are near their EOL and refresh them before they expire.

You would need to implement that in social auth pipeline or implement a custom backend for this.
Check the docs [here](http://psa.matiasaguirre.net/docs/pipeline.html#authentication-pipeline).

Hi @andy-maier, when you call `document.save()` it will update the `_rev` field of your existing document object and you'll be able to access the new revision with `doc.get('_rev')`.  
For example:
```
    doc = Document(db, 'doc-id')
    doc['new'] = 'value'
    assert doc.exists() is False
    assert doc.get('_rev') is None
    doc.create() #create the doc
    assert doc.exists() is True
    assert doc.get('_rev').startswith('1-')
    doc['new'] = 'newer value'
    doc.save() #update the 'new' field
    assert doc.get('_rev').startswith('2-')
```

```python
def topk(data=None, axis=_Null, k=_Null, ret_typ=_Null, is_ascend=_Null, name=None, attr=None, out=None, **kwargs):

    vals = []

        else:

    if data is not None: 
        assert isinstance(data, SymbolBase), \
            "Argument data must be Symbol instances, but got %s"%str(data)
        sym_kwargs['data'] = data

    if axis is not _Null:

I agree we should provide similar interfaces for this and 2bit right now, at least on the user facing API first.

A problem is that this solution requires creating a server for this dtype of 16bit. Which means we need to know type of compression when creating a kvstore. In the 2bit PR, since we still use float32 to represent values (for 2bit compression, 16 values represented in one float32), the original server was sent a message to know the type of compression.

The only way I see to merge interfaces is to have compression parameters even in my case to come in at the time of creating kvstore. To start this, I would like to understand how to give the argument defined in python/mxnet/kvstore_server.py to start the server.

General n-bit compression can be done. But neither this PR nor my PR can be directly extended as of now. In my PR, although the framework in kvstores can be generalized easily, the operator is 2bit specific. Either that can be extended by other having operators for different specific n, or if possible coming up with a generic operator.

My approach here is to KISS.  And yes in the future there will be some
breakage. But there's arguably more value in detecting the breakage,
understanding the variety of non-compliant systems and putting in explicit
fixes.  The alternative is that you'd never be sure as to whether the
configuability you've added preemptively is ever used and therefore whether
you could ever change it - since you don't know how/if it is being used.

For that reason I don't think the existing code (which assumed JSON
content) was a bad idea - it was good enough until the time it proved to be
insufficient and is now being updated because of a real need.

--
Michal Ostrowski

Sure I can provide an example but let me quickly give you some background.
A function decorated with `asyncio.coroutine` does not implement an `__await__` method. That method is called by `aiohttp` [directly](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/7f5d2066456e9f40b539e290f41d5c4b736389f7/aiohttp/client.py#L848) and raises the `AttributeError` I originally posted.

```python
>>> import asyncio
>>>
>>> @asyncio.coroutine
>>> def python34():
>>>    pass
>>> hasattr(python34(), '__await__')
False
>>> async def python35():
>>>    pass
>>> hasattr(python35(), '__await__')
True
```
This is the knowledge I based my changes on. Will try to post a gist to show a real example with aiohttp today.

It is right that my patch does not remove `asyncio.coroutine` in the linked location. That code handles the situation where a decorated function isn't a coroutine and I wonder why someone would use the async implementation of `backoff` in that case. I would either fall back to the synchronous `backoff` decorator or raise an exception that this decorator only works with async coroutine functions.
If this is all intentional and the use case of an async backoff decorating a normal function has to work I am fine to update the PR with a change to `async def` syntax.

There is something of a chicken and egg problem gaining contributors to a library with an unstable API.
Unstable APIs make it hard for people to use it in their large projects as they are then burdened with the risk of their code breaking at inopportune times.  If they cannot use it then they won't contribute to it either.

The risk factor is magnified for for-profit organizations because they have their livelihoods riding on the software they are writing.  Where I work there are many people who are interested in python/git integration.  Since the organization is large, I'm sure there are people that know enough C and python to be able to contribute back and help but I know I recommend *not* using pygit2 for any of the larger projects that make money for the company because dealing with its continuous API breakage makes it a pain to use in the long term.

This is not to disregard any of your work, I love it and admire it, but I can only use it for my personal projects until something more stable comes along.

I believe this also relates to the issue 230 about finding a source of funding to make work on pygit2 more sustainable.

Unfortunately I can't say the same about the state restore functionality which looks broken for Spotify tracks. There's a race condition between Spotify being ready ('Logged in to Spotify in online mode') and loading the track during playback state restoration. Your log clearly shows Mopidy trying to load the Spotify track before the Spotify backed was ready. I guess whether or not you'll run into this depends on the network and possibly also if the track has been previously played (it may still be cached and therefore already "loaded").

I don't think we currently have any mechanism which allows slow to initialise backends to signal core they are really ready - maybe we should. As an alternative, we could move pyspotify's [connection state check](https://github.com/mopidy/pyspotify/blob/v2.x/develop/spotify/utils.py#L231-L234) into the [timeout case](https://github.com/mopidy/pyspotify/blob/v2.x/develop/spotify/utils.py#L245-L246) so we give it an opportunity to become ready; a bit of a hack. Or maybe have mopidy-spotify's [on_change](https://github.com/mopidy/mopidy-spotify/blob/develop/mopidy_spotify/playback.py#L47-L89) function wait with a timeout on a new `SpotifyBackend._is_ready` threading `Event` that gets set by `SpotifyBackend.on_logged_in()`.

With a recent fix to pyspotify, you can now run the following not use the libspotify cache and show debug timings from Mopidy-Spotify:

```
mopidy -o loglevels/mopidy_spotify=debug -o spotify/cache_dir=
```

As `__del__()` is not reliably called in python 3, the tests leak heavily as no repository it ever used gets cleaned up, leaving plenty of the processes shown above. Each of them comes with three pipes, quickly using up the processes resources.

For example, it's possible to run all tests with `ulimit -n 64` in python 2, but needs `ulimit -n 140` in python 3.

If I'm correct about there not being a bug (obviously if the extension
fails to load on CentOS 5 because of a symbol resolution error from the
dynamic linker, then it's back to the drawing board), the source of your
confusion is that the referenced version of `glibc` doesn't need to comply
with PEP 513 at all -- it's the versioned reference *itself* that needs to
comply. The most readable documentation for how the symbol resolution
process works is probably https://software.intel.com/
sites/default/files/m/a/1/e/dsohowto.pdf, but the key here is that the
Python extension module or another shared library inside your wheel has
undefined external references to symbols like `open@GLIBC_2.2.5`, and we
need to check that it's referencing an old enough version of the symbol.
But when you recurse and actually load up that version of glibc, you're
just noticing that it provides these symbols, as well as more recently
versioned ones. That's fine -- the requirement is only that your library
doesn't references these more recent symbols.

> Is there a reason your package manager can't have pre-built versions of these packages available or vendor them itself (into the setuptools build recipe)?

In general, yes, there is a reason. Our package manager is designed to install software in the world of HPC, where you may need to use exotic compilers like xlc to install something on a bluegene-q or cray supercomputer. Since we need to support hundreds of operating systems and dozens of compilers, we haven't spent much time on supporting binary package installation like Python's wheels or Homebrew's taps. We have plans to do so in the future, but it has been low priority. Obviously, the compiler/OS doesn't matter much for non-compiled packages like setuptools, but the mechanism would have to be the same.

We have dealt with circular dependencies before. For example, pkg-config depends on glib and vice versa. Luckily the developers realized this, and pkg-config comes with it's own internal copy of glib, much like setuptools used to come with its dependencies. This can be annoying since we end up having to add the same patches for glib to both packages, but it prevents a circular dependency which is nice.

We could theoretically vendor the dependencies ourselves. That seems like the easiest solution to me if we ever run into a setuptools dependency where `distutils.core` is insufficient to handle the installation. I see a lot of packages that use `distutils.core` as a fallback. Out of curiousity, how common is it to find a package that uses features of `setuptools` that are not available in `distutils.core`?

The `.pypirc` file applies to more than just setuptools. It's used by distutils and [twine](/pypa/twine) and probably others.

But more importantly, the purpose and location of the file is [defined in Python itself](https://docs.python.org/3.1/distutils/uploading.html), and the purpose is meant to be cross-project and to store settings that are associated with the user and not the project.

So before we go adding new interfaces to tweak how one might use this file, I'd rather explore what use-case you have that's leading you to the need to have a .pypirc at all. For example, setuptools itself runs its tests in Travis-CI, so not Jenkins, but it does incorporate automated deployments all without the use of .pypirc.

In my organization, we do use Jenkins, and there we don't do automated deployments, but our manual deployments also don't rely on .pypirc. Instead, they simply indicate the index url in their upload command in setup.cfg:

```
[upload]
repository = https://devpi.example.com/root/repo/
```

Then, each user may have a `.pypirc` which indicates the username for that repo (and Jenkins could have a username which is relevant to its uploads):

```
[distutils]
index-servers =
    pypi
    myco

[pypi]
repository: https://upload.pypi.org/legacy/
username: jaraco

[myco]
repository: https://devpi.example.com/root/repo/
username: jaraco
```

Would an approach like this work for your use-case?

Changing the shebang of installed scripts to “env python” means that they will work only in an activated virtualenv.  The scripts were generated with explicit absolute paths so that they would always use the Python in the venv, and thus find the installed packages needed by the scripts.

*sigh* Yeah. The way we handle keyword parameters in Python makes it really inconvenient to add new "special" parameters. We already have a few of those (`api_key`, `stripe_version`, `stripe_account`, `idempotency_key`) and they need to be present on every request method's signature (e.g. [here](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/blob/f948b8b95b6df5b57c7444a05d6c83c8c5e6a0ac/stripe/api_resources/abstract/createable_api_resource.py#L10-L11)).

I would really rather avoid introducing new special parameters, esp. since this one as you pointed out would just be a configuration flag not directly related to the API. How about introducing a context manager for this? E.g.:

```python
with stripe.catch_responses() as r:
    charge = stripe.Charge.retrieve("ch_...")
    request_id = r[0].headers['Request-Id']
```

Basically, the context manager would collect the responses to all requests sent within the context.

don't think it needs to be perfect.

Padding is required before applying the Fourier transform when doing
convolutions, so you have more data to work with.  But then you also
have a very efficient algorithm, probably implemented well in a
low-level language.  It may also be worth double checking whether
fftconvolve intelligently pads to sizes for which the FFT can be
executed rapidly (so called smooth numbers, see
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooley%E2%80%93Tukey_FFT_algorithm).

About output ranges:

Well, I checked them, but at least for me, the use of preserve_range is ambiguous.
"    preserve_range : bool, optional
        Whether to keep the original range of values. Otherwise, the input
        image is converted according to the conventions of `img_as_float`."

And in "img_as_float":
"The range of a floating point image is [0.0, 1.0] or [-1.0, 1.0] when converting from unsigned or signed datatypes, respectively. If the input image has a float type, intensity values are not modified and can be outside the ranges [0.0, 1.0] or [-1.0, 1.0]."

So let's check the possibilities:
- preserve_range=false, img=uint8 (max value = 192)
  -> the output can be any integer between 0 and 255

- preserve_range=false, img=float (max value = 0.75)
  the input is converted to [0,0.75]
  -> the output can be any float

- preserve_range=false, img=float (max value = 1.5)
  -> the output can be any float 

- preserve_range=true, img=uint8 (min value = 10, max value = 192)
  -> the output can be any integer between 10 and 192? or 0-255?

- preserve_range=true, img=float (min value = 0.1, max value = 0.75)
  -> the output can be any float between 0, - 1? or 0.1 , 0.75?

- preserve_range=true, img=float (min value = -0.1, max value = 1.5)
  -> the output can be any float between -0.1 and 1.5? or -1 and 1?

My interpretation is this:
- preserve_range= true: 
    - any integer type: it can use the whole integer domain, e.g. uint8 input with 192 max value, could produce result with maximum value of 255.
    - any float input between [0,1] or [-1,1] will keep this range. So if the input had only [0,0.5] range, then the output still can be [0,1]
    - any float input outside [-1,1] or [0,1] can have a range of [min(image), max(image)]???
    
- preserve_range= false: 
  - integers: the same 
  - floats: any output? regardless of its smaller or larger than 1.0?

So could someone clarify the expected behavior, including edge cases?

Here is a simple example, in case the information above is somehow not enough:
```
import numpy as np
from skimage.transform import radon, iradon
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


img_orig = np.zeros((10, 10))
img_orig[2:4, 2:4] = 1
img_orig[7:9, 2:4] = 1

# Case 1. Reference sinogram
# sinogram = radon(img_orig, theta=[0, 90], circle=True)

# Case 2. "Your sinogram"
proj_0 = np.zeros((10, 1))
proj_90 = np.zeros((10, 1))
proj_0[2:4, :] = 2
proj_90[2:4, :] = 1
proj_90[7:9, :] = 1
sinogram = np.hstack((proj_0, proj_90))

img_rec = iradon(sinogram, theta=[0, 90], output_size=10)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(ncols=2)
axes[0].matshow(img_orig, cmap=plt.cm.Greys_r, interpolation='none')
axes[1].matshow(img_rec, cmap=plt.cm.Greys_r, interpolation='none')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

> "min" and "max" (rather than "fmin" and "fmax", applies to integers)

> Probably it would be better if I just removed fmin and fmax because they are sort-of reductions but not numpy-like reductions. NumExpr 2.6 has reductions, so the lack of them in Ne3 is a known regression, and these two functions can be achieved with where.

Min and max can be reducers, but they can also be mappers when they act like `where(a > b, a, b)`. A user is more likely to want to type `max(a, b)` or might not think of the "where" construction. I'd want to keep them, especially for more than two arguments.

The "f" prefix (in fmin, fmax, fabs, and fmod) is unnecessary and probably not what a user would guess. I think they exist to indicate floating point in a language without dispatch by argument type, possibly C. (I can't remember if dispatch by argument type is a C++ feature.) Maybe we need both for historical reasons, but Python users are more likely to want min, max, abs, and mod (if not just `%`).

Speaking of mod, is there an option for C/Java-style `%` versus Python-style `%`, which differ in how negative values are treated? Some languages provide both as "modulo" and "remainder".

The reason I find is that, raven will build a thread to consume the log, which will be start after first log print statement. The code is in `raven/transport/threaded.py`
When I first print log in main process, it runs the `get_worker` method in `ThreadHTTPTransport`, and construct a `AsyncWorker` object. This object will start the consume thread when it is constructed. Then I start my child process. Child process will keep the memory as main process, so the `AsyncWorker` object still exist. But the construct method hasn't been called, and there is no thread consume the log message queue.

Yes, the pixelization is the source of the imprecision in these algorithms.

I don't currently do any iteration.  I did some accuracy tests on Gaussians and Exponentials where I know the exact values.  For FWHM, I found that using the Nyquist scale, which is the default for GalSim's drawImage routine was accurate to.much less than 1%.  And for the moments, it was accurate to a few percent.  So in these two cases, I just let the default scale be Nyquist.

For HLR, on the tests I did, it was only good to a little worse than 10 percent with the Nyquist scale, but using 1/2 that was accurate to a few percent.  So I made that the default.  Going to around 0.03 * Nyquist was good to 4 dp.

I'm inclined to just document that the default pixel scale is only expected to be accurate to a few percent, possibly worse depending on the profile, and tell the user how to improve on this if they desire (by specifying a smaller scale value).  The routines are already not super fast, so I'm not sure I want to have the default be a complicated iterative procedure to achieve a given accuracy.

It's definitely an IE issue and I think it has to do with some of the `IS_IE` calculations being performed. Specifically, the issue arises with the `transform` property. 

Currently, the inline style on the `span.fa-layers-text` comes out with the following CSS transform:
```
transform: translate(-8em, -8em) scale(0.25) rotate(0deg)
```
This ends up shooting the text way off from where it is intended to be. 

Manually switching the CSS over to the following seems to get it a little bit closer so I wonder if it would handle it better to take the value being applied to the `em`, if any x/y transforms are applied then plus/minus those, the divide the result by the UNITS_IN_GRID property and using that as a percentage.
```
transform: translate(-50%, -50%) scale(0.25) rotate(0deg)
```

With a transform of `{ y: 2 }` then you would end up with the following (accurate for my use case):
```
transform: translate(-50%, -37.5%) scale(0.25) rotate(0deg)
```

_Note: These are all back of the napkin calculations that don't account for everything obviously..._

Answering my own questions (after talking to @CyberDem0n) the idea of this is to make sure the replica can start after the master changes some of those controldata-mentioned parameters (i.e. max_connections), since WAL is replayed asynchronously and may potentially still have the old value of the parameter when the new value is applied to the replica. Therefore, once the WALs are replayed up to the point when the new value of the parameter takes effect, we need to make sure the same value of the desired parameter as on the master is in force; therefore, we need to set pending_restart as a reminder to restart the replica and apply the value from the global configuration (otherwise, the replica will continue to run with the parameters taken from pg_controldata the very first time it crashes by observing non-matching values in the WAL).

I'd suggest adding some of it as a comment to the `_build_effective_configuration`.

I've been originally approaching this from the standpoint of doing a custom bootstrap from the WAL archive, which would produce the very first node in the cluster that might be wrongly configured and, therefore, is unable to restore the WAL it is being bootstrapped with, however, this is not addressed by this PR.

Looked into the aforementioned hang today. One major issue is that some really excessively big expression trees are being generated by the time the calldatacopy. i mostly looked was looking at what calldataload returns. when calldataload executes, `self.data` represents the tx data. this is an ArrayProxy object, that wraps over an already massive expression tree object composed of ArrayStores and ArraySelects. It is big due to various code like this https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/master/manticore/platforms/evm.py#L2290-L2294 which loops over an ArrayProxy performing assignments. every write builds another layer on the tree, and so our trees get hundreds of layers deep immediately, based on the length of data.

then when calldataload executes, the tree grows every bigger when the line https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/master/manticore/platforms/evm.py#L1586 slices the arrayproxy'd tree. i don't totally understand what this does yet. 

the excessive size of these trees is definitely a problem, and i suspect we could definitely be more intelligent in generating our trees. however, it's not totally clear to me that fixing this problem is actually what we need to do, as the deeper problem remains that the `total_space` argument below is symbolic and currently we do eager solving. a couple thoughts for more thorough solutions

- use lazy solving instead of eager solving, as i believe #771 will allow
- preconstrain the data, if we know the function prototypes ahead of time. we would basically be doing what _concretize_offsets_and_sizes does, but before launching the tx, not after.

fixing the smt expr trees size is probably easier than those two, and we should see if that helps the issue first.

---

also, here's a little pseudocode snippet of the main evm code that's causing issues:

```
offset = read_input(4)
nelements = read_input(offset + 4) 
total_space = 32 * nelements
copy(dst, src, total_space)
```
(references issue #442)

@dhermes @daspecster To follow up on our offline discussion:

* The GAPIC API object constructor takes as [an optional parameter](https://github.com/googleapis/api-client-staging/blob/cf19fe151c9fd865936b2c1972fcc9974f6add35/generated/python/gapic-google-logging-v2/google/cloud/gapic/logging/v2/logging_service_v2_api.py#L147) a retry configuration object ([example in JSON](https://github.com/googleapis/gax-python/blob/master/google/gax/api_callable.py#L311); use a Python dict in the constructor instead).
* This dict allows you to override the default retrying behavior for all GAPIC methods. You can pass a partial configuration (i.e., configure some but not all of the methods in an API).
* Generally, by default, GAPIC methods retry automatically if the corresponding HTTP call uses a verb that is idempotent.
* Every GAPIC package contains in the package data the default configuration ([example](https://github.com/googleapis/api-client-staging/blob/cf19fe151c9fd865936b2c1972fcc9974f6add35/generated/python/gapic-google-logging-v2/google/cloud/gapic/logging/v2/logging_service_v2_client_config.json)).
* You can change the retrying behavior on a per-method-call (as opposed to per-method) basis using the [GAX `CallOptions` object](https://github.com/googleapis/gax-python/blob/master/google/gax/__init__.py#L182). Every GAPIC method takes a `CallOptions` object as an optional parameter.

IMO it's nice to have a possibility to change the default value for all calls. I also increase default retries. For the old `google-api-python-client` I use own subclass of `googleapiclient.http.HttpRequest` with the overridden `execute` method.

@dhermes here's what that snippet I posted in my first comment evolved into over the past week:

```python
# The maximum number of retries that should be done per Datastore
# error code.
_MAX_RETRIES_BY_CODE = {
    grpc.StatusCode.INTERNAL: 1,
    grpc.StatusCode.ABORTED: 5,  # Only retried for non-transactional commits
    grpc.StatusCode.UNAVAILABLE: 5,
    grpc.StatusCode.DEADLINE_EXCEEDED: 5,
}


def _handle_errors(f, transactional=False):
    @functools.wraps(f)
    def handler(*args, **kwargs):
        retries = 0
        while True:
            try:
                return f(*args, **kwargs)
            # TODO: Replace w/ concrete error types once they are
            # added to gcloud.  See: google-cloud-python/issues/2583
            except (
                google.cloud.exceptions.Conflict,  # gcloud catches ABORTED
                google.cloud.exceptions._Rendezvous
            ) as e:
                if isinstance(e, google.cloud.exceptions.Conflict):
                    code = grpc.StatusCode.ABORTED
                else:
                    code = e.code()

                max_retries = _MAX_RETRIES_BY_CODE.get(code)
                if max_retries is None or transactional and code == grpc.StatusCode.ABORTED:
                    raise

                if retries > max_retries:
                    raise RetriesExceeded(e)

                backoff = min(0.0625 * 2 ** retries, 1.0)
                bread.get_logger().debug("Sleeping for %r before retrying failed request...", backoff)

                retries += 1
                time.sleep(backoff)

    return handler


class Client(datastore.Client):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(Client, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        self.delete_multi = _handle_errors(self.delete_multi)
        self.get_multi = _handle_errors(self.get_multi)
        self.put_multi = _handle_errors(self.put_multi)

    def transaction(self, *args, **kwargs):
        transaction = super(Client, self).transaction(*args, **kwargs)
        transaction.commit = _handle_errors(transaction.commit, transactional=True)
        return transaction

    def query(self, *args, **kwargs):
        query = super(Client, self).query(*args, **kwargs)
        query.fetch = _handle_errors(query.fetch)
        return query
```

We have been running this in our staging environment for about a week now and this seems to have cut the error rate down to nearly zero (we see a `_Rendezvous` with code `UNAUTHENTICATED` get raised every couple of days -- not sure what to make of that yet). In production, we've been running gcloud versions `<=0.17` with `gcloud_requests` for quite a while now and that employs [the same error handling model][gcr]. Our error rate in production is 0 as most retries succeed immediately and we've never gone over the retry limit (lowest is 1, highest is 5) for any of the error codes.

This is the set of Datastore methods we use in our ORM which we've seen raise at least one of these errors:

* `datastore.Client.{delete,get,put}_multi`
* `datastore.Query.fetch`
* `datastore.Query.next_page`
* `datastore.Transaction.commit`

I suspect `datastore.Transaction.rollback` might also need to be patched, but we do that infrequently enough in the staging environment that it hasn't affected us yet so we haven't wrapped it. At any rate, under a production workload all of these methods will definitely end up raising one of these (retriable) errors (quite frequently!) so, if the library doesn't handle them automatically, end users have two options:

1) either they wrap the library and expose a safe interface like we have, or
2) they handle retries at every call site

Personally, I believe number 2 does not scale in large codebases/orgs. I think the first option is perfectly reasonable, but it has the disadvantage that learned experiences are less likely to be shared between users whereas if retries were consolidated in the library (perhaps with an option to turn them off or configure max retries and backoff figures) it would lead to more reliable code across the board.

Regarding your [changes][changes], I notice in some cases multiple error codes map to the same exception and the exceptions don't keep track of the original code. This means that a retriable error code like `Internal` and [I believe] a non-retriable error code like `DATA_LOSS` both end up mapping to `InternalServerError`, so user code can't reliably determine if it should retry or not. The same is true of `ABORTED` and `ALREADY_EXISTS` since they both map to `Conflict`.

[gcr]: https://github.com/LeadPages/gcloud_requests/blob/master/gcloud_requests/requests_connection.py#L169
[changes]: https://github.com/dhermes/google-cloud-python/commit/3e2788c773408b995c7cb5b2834015819bc941ae

Thanks for the pointer.  From a quick read… it seems that this module doesn't actually pickle `traceback` objects, but builds a `traceback` proxy, which behaves very similarly.  So it needs some work before I'd stick it in `dil`… however, it's worth looking into further.  The main issue with picking a `traceback` is that it depends on a `frame` -- which is intimately tied to the `GIL`… and thus, doesn't pickle for the same reason as a `generator` doesn't pickle (i.e. a `frame` is not serializable because the `GIL` is not copyable).  The proper way to do this is to pickle a `frame`… and the library referenced above sidesteps that by creating it's own `frame` object -- ultimately, not the right thing to do.

The recommendation for adding new stuff to maven_layout is to write your own plugin. There is an example in the docs:

https://pantsbuild.github.io/howto_plugin.html


Simple Configuration
¶
If you want to extend Pants without adding any 3rd Party libraries that aren't already referenced by Pants, you can use the following technique using sources stored directly in your repo. All you need to do is add the directory where your plugin sources are stored to PYTHONPATH and put the package where the plugin is defined in pants.ini.

In the example below, the stock maven_layout() function will be extended by adding extra source root definitions and library types.

Define a home for your plugins. In this example we'll use a directory named 'plugins'

Create an empty plugins/ext_maven_layout/__init__.py to define your python package.

Create a python module plugins/ext_maven_layout/ext_maven_layout.py that creates a new function to enhance maven_layout():

# ext_maven_layout/ext_maven_layout.py
import os

from pants.backend.codegen.targets.java_wire_library import JavaWireLibrary
from pants.backend.jvm.targets.jar_library import JarLibrary
from pants.backend.maven_layout.maven_layout import maven_layout
from pants.base.source_root import SourceRoot

def ext_maven_layout(parse_context, basedir=''):
 """Sets up typical maven project source roots for all built-in pants target types.

 See maven_layout() defined in the pants source code. Appends additional roots and targets
 to the stock version.
 """
 def root(path, *types):
   SourceRoot.register_mutable(os.path.join(parse_context.rel_path, basedir, path), *types)

 # Use the stock maven_layout to get started
 maven_layout(parse_context, basedir=basedir)

 # Add additional targets to existing source roots
 root('src/main/java', JarLibrary)
 root('src/test/java', JarLibrary)

 # Add additional source roots
 root('src/main/wire_proto', JavaWireLibrary, JarLibrary)
 root('src/test/wire_proto', JavaWireLibrary, JarLibrary)
Create plugins/ext_maven_layout/register.py to register the functions in your plugin. When registering a backend in pants.ini, register.py is used by the pants plugin api to register new functions exposed in build files, targets, tasks and goals:

# plugins/ext_maven_layout/register.py

from pants.base.build_file_aliases import BuildFileAliases
from ext_maven_layout.ext_maven_layout import ext_maven_layout

def build_file_aliases():
 return BuildFileAliases.create(
   context_aware_object_factories={
    'ext_maven_layout': BuildFileAliases.curry_context(ext_maven_layout)
   }
 )
Update your pants wrapper script to include the plugins/ directory on PYTHONPATH:

PYTHONPATH=plugins:${PYTHONPATH}
export PYTHONPATH
In pants.ini, add your new plugins directory to the list of backends to load when pants starts. This instructs pants to look for a module named ext_maven_layout.register and invoke it.

[DEFAULT]
backend_packages: [
    "ext_maven_layout",
  ]

A few notes.

* Neither solution would seem to rule out having a `--fail=(fast|slow)` flag at some point.
* Switching to failing `slow` by default would work for basic cases.
  * Would mean waiting for the runtime to go completely idle before forking (although waiting for the runtime to go idle and then shutting it down is racey, so would need retries).
    * But the implementation of a user-facing `--fail=slow` would be a bit different than what is described here, because you wouldn't want to wait for "all" running requests to complete: just your own.
  * The daemon lifecycle lock currently prevents multiple concurrent requests in the daemon, and so if we complete all Nodes in the graph before forking, nothing will be running, and we'll shut down cleanly.
  * This would make removing the daemon lifecycle lock challenging for as long as we're forking (ie, until all tasks are `@console_rules`).
* Improving our ability to fail `fast` might be easier.
  * It would probably look like a rapid termination of all running work, similar to [invalidation](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/82cb6941c01038a3b6af44f149ef2369271bb442/src/rust/engine/graph/src/lib.rs#L151-L157), but only for things that are currently running.
  * Works fine in the presence of concurrency, since invalidation does too: a request running in another thread would see invalidation of Nodes it needed and retry.

Thanks @quiver. I'd forgotten that vim also does this.  Just as another data point, ipython has similar semantics (`!cd /tmp` doesn't change your directory but `%cd /tmp` does).  I know ipython has [been discussed before](https://github.com/awslabs/aws-shell/issues/19#issuecomment-161286510), but given that ipython's magics (as well as sqlite's dot commands) was a main source of inspiration for aws-shell dot commands, it's worth considering what approach they've taken.

That being said, I do share the same concerns about this not being discoverable.  I like @e1ven's [suggestion](https://github.com/awslabs/aws-shell/issues/72#issuecomment-165542168) of giving pointers to users to help them learn the differences.  Perhaps we could do something similar here: if the user provides a command of the basic form "!cd /single/directory" we would could still execute the command (which would do nothing) but warn them that they might have meant ".cd /single/directory".

Also, as an aside, what if we add a `.tutorial` dot command?  Then we check if it's the user's first time running the shell, we write out a message that tells them they can run `.tutorial` if they want a tour of the shell.  This is where we could tell them about `!` and dot commands, etc.

To whom is interested: I managed to wrap docker-py: https://github.com/tenforce/docker-py-aiohttp

It can certainly be ported to other asyncio libraries using the same principles. The core of the principles are located here: https://github.com/cecton/requests2aiohttp

In brief: I made a class that is meant to override `requests.sessions.Session`. All the HTTP calls are redirected to aiohttp and I have a custom `Response` class that wraps aiohttp's `Response` class. Its main purpose is to have a common API with `requests.sessions.Session` so the docker-py code can use it the same way.

It's not state of the art but I added integration tests for the same Docker versions tested by docker-py and it seems very stable.
