You can use either `com.schriftgestaltung.Keep Glyphs` (which is a list of glyph names that will be kept, the rest are dropped) in the global font's `lib.plist`, or the `com.schriftgestaltung.Glyphs.Export` boolean flag in a glyph's `lib` element.

If `com.schriftgestaltung.Keep Glyphs` key is not present or empty in the lib.plist, it's ignored (all glyphs are kept).

If `com.schriftgestaltung.Glyphs.Export` is not present, it defaults to True, which means you can add a False value for the glyphs you wish to drop.

I've been digging into fontmake to find a good spot to make the changes. `font.save(path)` is called from `save_otfs()` and `build_variable_font()`, both of which consult `_output_path()` and `_output_dir()` on various occasions, where the former is what's used to write the actual binaries. Why are there two methods for this?

My idea would be to change `_output_path()` to look for a `self.output_dir` variable (instead of modifying all callers) and generating one with `_output_dir()` if it's not available. The argument would have to be passed to FontProject.__init__ and deleted from the args dict so `save_otfs` doesn't trip. Are there more elegant ideas?

Is that intentional? This might be artificially inflating the overlap between reproducibility tests if the only deviations are between frames 3 and 4, and are diluted with overlap between 1, 2, 3 and 4, 5 (knocking reproducibility down a few percentage points). (This may also help address the size issue.)

I think the check to make sure trackpy is finding the correct number of features in the correct place (static image test), and a check to make sure it is tracking those features in the right way (moving image test), should happen separately. The fact that there are several things breaking the reproducibility test indicates that that one test might be doing too much. If you look at the setup of the `TestReproducibility` class in `test_reproducibility.py`, the process of extracting an image sequence, batching them to find features, and linking the dataframes are all done in one go, and intermediate results are not saved. If we save and test each step individually, that might improve the test.

The `batch()` (or, `locate()`) function is also tested in `test_feature()`, and ideally this suite of test would have failed as well. But those tests are based on generating artificial spots at specified locations, adding noise, and comparing the detected spot locations (the gold standard is mathematically verifiable), whereas the test that failed was based on comparing to an "arbitrary" standard (which was only verifiable with a prior version of the software using an erroneous filter implementation), so I don't think those tests can be improved.

In the near-ish future, Binder will support the new `pipfile` configuration which will make this a lot simpler.

`exclude` was dropped in the `1.0.0` refactor.  I recommend creating a separate RLP type for the mining hash:

```python
class MiningHeader(rlp.Serializable):
    fields = [
        ('parent_hash', hash32),
        ('uncles_hash', hash32),
        ('coinbase', address),
        ('state_root', trie_root),
        ('transaction_root', trie_root),
        ('receipt_root', trie_root),
        ('bloom', int256),
        ('difficulty', big_endian_int),
        ('block_number', big_endian_int),
        ('gas_limit', big_endian_int),
        ('gas_used', big_endian_int),
        ('timestamp', big_endian_int),
        ('extra_data', binary),
    ]

# or if you want to avoid the duplication of the fields:
class MiningHeader(rlp.Serializable):
    fields = Header._meta.fields[:-2]
```

The VUnit scanner identifies testbenches as entities with a `runner_cfg` generic. In your case there are two such files so VUnit thinks you have one testbench without any test cases (which is ok) and one testbench with one test case. `python run.py --list` will give

``` console
lib.stimulus_response.OSVVM_tests
lib.tb.all
Listed 2 tests
```
If you change the name of `runner_cfg` in `stimulus_response.vhd` to something else like `nested_runner_cfg` you will get this when running the same command

``` console
lib.tb.all
Listed 1 tests
```
VUnit assumes that your test cases are defined in the same file as the `runner_cfg` generic. If not  you have to add the following to `run.py`

``` python
tb = lib.entity("tb");
tb.scan_tests_from_file("stimulus_response.vhd")
```

Run the command again and you will have what you want

``` console
lib.tb.OSVVM_tests
Listed 1 tests
```

Note that the `Test_Random_Valid_Transactions` procedure ends with a `wait` such that the simulation will stop with nothing more to do. A stop without passing `test_runner_cleanup` is considered an error even though there were no failing asserts. The reason is to detect errors like division by zero which cause simulations stops despite that there was no failing asserts.

Hi @j08lue,

I apologize for the late response. Here is why your delete request is failing:

When you perform an update_message operation with a visibility_timeout, another message object is returned with a new pop_receipt. Essentially, every time you set the visibility_timeout and hide the message from others, you get a pop_receipt from the service to guarantee that yourself still have visibility to the message within the timeout. So here is what you could do:

```python
list_result1 = self.qs.get_messages(queue_name, visibility_timeout=5)
message = self.qs.update_message(queue_name,
    list_result1[0].id,
    list_result1[0].pop_receipt,
    visibility_timeout=5)
self.qs.delete_message(queue_name, list_result1[0].id, message.pop_receipt)
```

I would to point out that the new `message` object returned contains only a new pop_receipt and not the message id. Please let me know if you have any other question.

@bluss The reasons why it won't be added to the notebook core is because:

* Users can accidentally hide the menu bar and "lock themselves out" if they don't know about the command palette or keyboard shortcut for showing the menu bar (as @takluyver mentioned)
* It introduces a UI change which is reserved for major versions (which aren't released very often)
* The notebook is currently in "support mode" meaning that we are not trying to add new features unless absolutely necessary

Nonetheless, the feature is available via [an extension](https://github.com/ipython-contrib/jupyter_contrib_nbextensions/tree/master/src/jupyter_contrib_nbextensions/nbextensions/hide_header) or a custom.js script.

That sounds like it's not saving before it offers you the document for download. We try to save, but we have to do it with a synchronous HTTP request, because downloading uses `window.open()`, which we can only call while handling a click event. You can work around it by explicitly saving (Ctrl+S, or click the save button in the toolbar) just before you download.

I see the same issue on try.jupyter.org using Firefox.

We may be able to get round this using a trick we have elsewhere: create a window while handling a click, and then set its location in a separate callback.

I'm glad this has generated some discussion. Before embarking on the rewrite, I opened #369 but got no comments there.

The reason for turning error messages into something more complex than just strings is my belief that strings are inadequate representations of error items.

Consider:

```
"The value should be greater than or equal to 5."
```

vs

```
{
    "constraint": "NUMBER_GTE",
    "parameter": 5,
}
```

The latter is obviously easier to parse.

Sure, in some situations we can simply pass on the English-language string and be done with it, but I think the structured approach is much better when building APIs and localized applications. Or what do you guys think?

That said, we could certainly include a utility to perform practical transformations on an error object, such as the ability to convert nested objects into Python primitives. Another one that I've thought about before is something to turn the tree into a series of JSON Pointer -like paths to the invalid items.

@jcnelson Yes.  `https://explorer.blockstack.org/insight-api/addr/{address}/utxo` 

It is off-the-shelf insight-api: https://github.com/bitpay/insight-api

We decided to not implement fast-sync for search and instead make search a separate subsystem.

@jcnelson, @shea256 and @larrysalibra here are new requirements: 

- Normally users will be pointed to search.blockstack.org (a public server) by default.
- The public server will have information/links for how to deploy their own node. 
- Initially, we will have a simple http://search.blockstack.org/v1/search?query=wenger interface
- Later, we'll merge the interface with the Blockstack API so you can turn search on and the v1/search end point will appear along with the rest of the Blockstack API.

If you're using a recent version of `signalfx-python`, the default timeout is now 5 seconds, which will definitely help here. If you can't upgrade, you can still change the timeout used when sending datapoints to SignalFx's ingest API by passing the `timeout` parameter either to the `SignalFx()` constructor, or when calling `.ingest()` to get the ingest client:

```python
import signalfx
sfx = signalfx.SignalFx().ingest(timeout=5)
```

Thanks @Cadair. A couple notes:

* Please update the docstrings of any methods whose arguments you change to
  reflect the new arguments even if those arguments are strictly optional.
* The more I think about this, the less I like having `user_id` as a
  separate kwarg on each method. We should just put a `query_params` kwarg
  on each method instead. This would also get rid of all the `timestamp`
  kwargs littered everywhere. This gives at least 2 benefits:
  1. The method signatures more closely match the api (information going in
     the headers is separate from information going in query_params is
     separate from the json payload... mostly).
  2. Any future (optional) extensions to the CS api involving query_params
     are already accounted for.
* We should include a note in each docstring listing possible contents of
  the `query_params` kwarg (e.g. "ts" and "user_id").

Historically, it's always been up to the subordinate charm author to ensure that their charm doesn't conflict with the principal.  The `use_venv` option was added to make that easier, but due to concerns about ensuring that the venv was activated properly during debug sessions or in bash charms (i.e., whenever the `charms.reactive` CLI is used), it wasn't made the default.  I think we could probably come up with a shim `charms.reactive` CLI that checks for the current hook environment and activates the proper venv, if available.  If we can do that, I'm +1 for making `use_venv` the default.  In the meantime, we can certainly add something to layer-basic (where the `use_venv` option is handled) to detect if it's not using `use_venv` (perhaps only for subordinates at first?) during build time and warn the charm author that it's a problem.

The JoinGroupRequest can block up to session.timeout.ms on the broker, so if the client uses a lower timeout than that to time out its request waiting for a response this might happen.

Now, this isnt exactly what is happening because the client knows that the JoinGroupRequest can take up to session.timeout.ms (and some grace period above that), but any other requests in-flight behind will be head-of-line-blocked and not processed by the broker until the JoinGroupRequest is done, so it is likely some other request that is actually timing out, such as a Metadata request, Fetch, etc.

The workaround is simple enough: keep socket.timeout.ms above session.timeout.ms.

And I would advise against having a low socket timeout anyway, a TCP connection may stall for more than a couple of seconds if any of the two systems connected are swapping, GC:ing, IO:peaking, etc, or if there is a shorter network outage / re-routing it may take some time for TCP to recover from retransmitting lost packets.

Also setting termencoding to UTF-8 is not needed:

1. If locale is UTF-8 then nothing needs to be done at all.
2. If locale is not UTF-8, but terminal thinks it is (common if terminal emulator itself was launched with `LANG=….UTF-8`, but Vim or shell was launched with `LANG=C` for some reasons) setting &encoding is *enough* because &termencoding defaults to empty (== “use the value from &encoding”).
3. In other cases you will see broken output.

So having `set encoding=utf-8` is enough.

Yes, it is a limitation of current design: on schema instantiation all field objects are bound to that particular instance. Through this binding they obtain context. If you will create multiple schemas, all field objects will bind to the last one created.

Solution: do not create multiple schemas, reuse a single instance.

Hi @vpndemand, if I understand your question correctly, you want to use a different folder for each label (with the name of the folder representing the label) as the data for an image classifier. For this task, I would do something like this. Make a folder structure like this, for separating the images by label:

```
➜  ~ find images
images
images/cat
images/cat/cat1.jpg
images/cat/cat2.jpg
images/dog
images/dog/dog2.jpg
images/dog/dog1.jpg
```

Then, in Turi Create, you can load these images and manipulate the `path` column to get the label:

```python
import turicreate as tc
sf = tc.image_analysis.load_images('images')
"""
sf is:
+-------------------------------+--------------------------+
|              path             |          image           |
+-------------------------------+--------------------------+
| /Users/zach/images/cat/cat... | Height: 1199 Width: 1200 |
| /Users/zach/images/cat/cat... |  Height: 400 Width: 600  |
| /Users/zach/images/dog/dog... |  Height: 486 Width: 729  |
| /Users/zach/images/dog/dog... |  Height: 455 Width: 680  |
+-------------------------------+--------------------------+
"""
sf['label'] = sf['path'].apply(lambda x: x[len('/Users/zach/images/'):])
"""
sf is:
+-------------------------------+--------------------------+--------------+
|              path             |          image           |    label     |
+-------------------------------+--------------------------+--------------+
| /Users/zach/images/cat/cat... | Height: 1199 Width: 1200 | cat/cat1.jpg |
| /Users/zach/images/cat/cat... |  Height: 400 Width: 600  | cat/cat2.jpg |
| /Users/zach/images/dog/dog... |  Height: 486 Width: 729  | dog/dog1.jpg |
| /Users/zach/images/dog/dog... |  Height: 455 Width: 680  | dog/dog2.jpg |
+-------------------------------+--------------------------+--------------+
"""
sf['label'] = sf['label'].apply(lambda x: x[:x.find('/')])
"""
sf is:
+-------------------------------+--------------------------+-------+
|              path             |          image           | label |
+-------------------------------+--------------------------+-------+
| /Users/zach/images/cat/cat... | Height: 1199 Width: 1200 |  cat  |
| /Users/zach/images/cat/cat... |  Height: 400 Width: 600  |  cat  |
| /Users/zach/images/dog/dog... |  Height: 486 Width: 729  |  dog  |
| /Users/zach/images/dog/dog... |  Height: 455 Width: 680  |  dog  |
+-------------------------------+--------------------------+-------+
"""
```

Then, you can proceed to train using the `label` column as the label. Hope this helps! Please reactivate this issue if your question is not answered, or open a new issue if you have a different question, thanks!

You should definitely scale your input data. Just experiment with getting the right value; between 0 and 1 is a good start.

This is not as much about overfitting as about making sure the training doesn't diverge: inputs that are large will likely lead to poor fit or outright numerical failures.

`predict_rank` will return a (num_users x num_items) matrix, where nonzero `(i, j)` entries contain ranks of item `j` for user `i`. You specify which these should be by passing in a `test_interactions` matrix.

In general returning a _dense_ matrix of this form is infeasible: for many problems, it would require a ludicrous amount of memory. If that's what you'd like, I'd recommend just iteratively calling `predict` for every user.

OK. I understand that we could manipulate the goals through the API. However, such a solution would not be generally available to others.

Since I have thought it through now, I go on to present my ideas about two sorting tactics that might generally be useful


Motivation
----------
The splitting tactic `split-clause` is and the hypothetical splitting tactic `partition` would be very useful for splitting a given goal (wrapping a CSP) into subgoals (each wrapping a sub-CSP). The combiners `then` and `par-then` are, and the hypothetical tactic `par-do` would be very useful for operating on subgoals, particularly when applying one of the solvers at the very end. However, the order of subgoals is arbitrary, depending on the order in which constraints were asserted.

Consider a strategy such as

`(check-sat-using (then split-clause smt))`

There are two points where we might wish to insert a sort tactic, namely before and after `split-clause.`.


sort-clauses
------------
Consider a hypothetical tactic `sort-clauses` as in the strategy 

`(check-sat-using (then sort-clauses split-clause smt))`

`sort-clauses` would sort the available clauses (within each goal) according to some criterion such as size. A simple metric measuring size would be to count the top-level constraints ci in the clause `(or c1 c2 ...)` that is picked by `split-clause`. Another useful parameter would be one controlling the sort direction in ascending or descending order.

So my base suggestion is a tactic

`(sort-clauses :ascending d)`

where the value of d could be true or false. Instead there could be two mutually exclusive parameters `:ascending` and `:descending`, which would make the tactic more readable.

If a tactic such as `sort-clauses` were in place, we could beneficially control the number of subgoals that are created by `split-clause`. We might be interested in splitting a small clause and consequently obtain only a few subgoals, or in splitting a large clause and obtain many subgoals.

Let us replace the tactic `then` by one of its parallel siblings.

`(check-sat-using (par-then (! sort-clauses :descending) split-clause smt))`

This strategy would pick one of the large clauses with a maximum number of elements and thereby create the maximum number n of subgoals. The tactic `par-then` would start n SMT-solvers working concurrently and competitvely on the subgoals. For this to work well, tactic `par-then` should probably have a parameter `:max-no-workers` that controls the maximum number or worker threads, as in

`(check-sat-using (! par-then (! sort-clauses :descending) :max-no-workers 6) split-clause smt))`

Thereby we would make full use of the computational power of our hardware (which in my case has 8 cores), without overloading it.


sort-goals
----------
Do we need anything else? I believe, yes.

After `split-clause` has done its work we might wish to intervene once more. `split-clause` produces a sequence of subgoals. The order of these subgoals is arbitrary, corresponding to the order of the elements in the clause that was split.

If we submit a sequence of subgoals to a bunch of solvers that concurrently and competitively work on the subgoals, as in the example above, we are interested in starting with easy to solve CSPs (subgoals) and work our way up to more difficult ones. Thus we increase our chances that an easy to solve CSP, wrapped into a subgoal, is solved early on, thereby cancelling the rest of the computations.

Let us threfore introduce a another hypothetical tactic dubbed `sort-goals`, as in

`(check-sat-using (par-then sort-clauses split-clause sort-goals smt))`

Of course, there is no metric that will tell us precisely how difficult a given CSP is for a given solver, expressed by how long it takes the solver to solve the CSP. However, in our experience the difficulty (in case of sat-CSPs) can reasonably well be estimated by computing a "size" of a CSP. 

Conceivable size metrics for goals are
- :no-of-constraints the number of constraints that make up the goal, counted at top-level (this number should be easy to get hold of)
- :no-of-variables the number of variables occuring in the constraints that make up the goal (requires to walk the constraints in order to collect the variables)
- :size some other metric measuring the size, e.g. a linear combination the two metrics above

Let us go with a linear parameter `:size`. Then it would be useful if there were two other parameters, namely
`:variable-weight` and `:constraint-weight`. The size of the CSP would be computed via

`:size = :variable-weight * :no-of-variables + :constraint-weight * :no-of-constraints`

Let us assume that the default values for the weights are 1 (which is what we always use).

Again a `:direction` parameter or equivalent would control whether the sorting should occur in ascending or descending order. 


Conclusion
----------
A strategy such as 

`(check-sat-using (par-then sort-clauses split-clause (! sort-goals :descending) smt))`

or, by using two other hypothetical tactics `partition` and `par-do`, a strategy such as  

`(check-sat-using (par-do partition (! sort-goals :descending) smt))`

would, in a few lines of concise "macro" code, accomplish a lot of what we have currently implemented in a lot of external Java code. Of course the sort tactics would first have to be implemented in Z3.

I believe that both sort tactics would be beneficial not only for the CSPs that we routinely solve, but for other Z3-users as well. Hopefully these will step forward and leave their encouraging comments.


Side remarks
------------
In our work we regularly partition a given CSP into minimal independent components, and usually want to try to solve the most difficult CSP-components first. Thereby we want to discover as early as possible whether these difficult components require human attention. The whole CSP-solution process may have to be halted, if the difficult components are too difficult for the solver. The process will be restarted only after we have suitably modified the CSP-components, so that the solver has a chance to solve them within some reasonable time limits.

@MattWindsor91 Thanks for giving this a try and using the CMake based build system to do so. I know ".NET core" exists but I don't have any familiarity with it. That being said I'll try my best to explain some of the problems you're seeing.

Looking at the steps you give it looks like you're fighting and hacking the build system rather than changing it to suit your needs.

The first thing I should point out that I did leave room in the CMake based build system for ".NET core" to be supported one day. Take a look at [cmake/modules/FindDotNetToolchain.cmake](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/cmake/modules/FindDotNetToolchain.cmake). This file is responsible for detecting the ".NET" toolchain. Currently it only supports the Windows ".NET" toolchain and Mono. The fact that you were able to successfully configure with `BUILD_DOTNET_BINDINGS` set to `ON` on macOS indicates you have Mono installed.

What needs to be done here is to add support for detecting if ".NET core" is installed and also provide an option to pick which toolchain to use when both Mono and ".NET core" are available (as in your case).

>  the reason I'm trying to do this is because I use Z3 (through z3x86win) in an F# project, and I'm trying to migrate it from Mono to .NET Core, without any success: the Microsoft.Z3.dll in z3x86win seems to work at first through the .NET Standard shims. but then tries to pull in kernel32.dll even if given a valid native z3 dylib.)

By `z3x86win` I assume you mean that you're using a built release of Z3 for 32-bit x86 Windows... but you're using macOS... I don't understand why you would expect that to work.

> project.json being the prerelease version of the .NET Core project file, one has to run dotnet migrate to get a .NET Core 1.x MSBuild.

This sounds like a ".NET core" specific thing. I do remember hearing about `project.json` files being a way of declaring projects but given that

* Z3 doesn't have this file is its repository.
* You seem to be doing some kind of migration of the `project.json` to the MSBuild format.

I can't really help with this. I don't understand why you would add the `project.json` and then immediately convert it to something else... 

> This MSBuild doesn't have version information---I'm not sure how to make cmake populate this, so I had to manually add it.

Looking at your build steps that can't really isn't going to work so well. You seem to be using a high level ".NET Core" build tool to try to build the .NET bindings but the CMake build system (and the previous Python build system) take a very low level approach and manually invoke the compiler directly.

> Using cmake/Ninja, nothing seems to copy the build/src/api/dotnet/*.cs files to somewhere the .NET Core builder can find them, causing lots of CS0246 errors for missing native API bridging. Copying these manually worked. I seem to have got this working before, so maybe it's an issue with the build generator I'm using...?

This is deliberate. The python (old build system) made the mistake of writing generated build files into the source tree. This causes loads of problems when trying to build multiple configurations from a single source tree, so instead the CMake build system emits generated build files into the build tree and leaves the source tree untouched.

The CMake build system could be taught to copy over all the `*.cs` files into the build tree too. However this hasn't been necessary so far because we invoke the C# compiler directly and pass it the absolute paths to all the source files. I don't think we should make the change you're suggesting because it introduces the problem of making sure the copied files are always up to date. This can be solved but it doesn't seem like the right thing to do given that I think that using ".NET Core"'s high level build tools is the wrong thing to do here.

Presumably it is possible to invoke ".NET Core's" C# compiler directly? If so then the approach to implement support properly into Z3's build system would be

* Modify `cmake/modules/FindDotNetToolchain.cmake` to detect ".NET Core"
* Modify `src/api/dotnet/CMakeLists.txt` to invoke the ".NET Core" compiler and make any other additional changes necessary.

If it is not possible to invoke ".NET Core's" C# compiler directly or there is some **huge** advantage to using ".NET Core"'s high level build tools then we need to take a different approach. My guess is that it would be something like this...

* Modify `cmake/modules/FindDotNetToolchain.cmake` to detect ".NET Core"
* Modify `src/api/dotnet/CMakeLists.txt` to do the follwing when the toolchain is ".NET Core"
  - Generate our own `csproj` file that contains the paths to all the source files and correct version information.
  - Set the build step to invoke `dotnet build` and then copy the result to the right location.

I'm not keen on this approach because it means we have a completely different code path for ".NET Core".


I don't have time to work on this but if you have the time to work on it I'm happy to provide advise on the CMake side of things.

For anyone else stuck on Eventlet 0.21, you can monkey patch GreenSocket:

```
############################
# Monkey patch Eventlet GreenThread to add context manager
# This is not required in eventlet 0.22 and up
from eventlet.greenio.base import GreenSocket
def add_enter(self):
    return self
def add_exit(self, *args):
    self.close()
GreenSocket.__enter__ = add_enter
GreenSocket.__exit__ = add_exit
######################
```

Quick report about how this is going:

The library is really easy to type. Excepting the IO, which seems to always be a pain.

I added `typing` as a requirement, because I thought it was nicer to use with some objects (like `Mode` being a `Container[Text]`). Where applicable, I used `typing.TYPE_CHECKING` to hide imports only used by the type checker.

I didn't change the API anywhere, except in `fs.multifs` where I turned `_required_writable` (which I renamed `_writable_required` to be more consistent with `_delegate_required`) into a method returning a filesystem instead of `None` to help typechecking:
```python
## before ##
self._writable_required()          # check write_fs is not None
self.write_fs.settext('...')       # we know this is not None, mypy doesn't !

## after ##
write_fs = self._writable_required()   # this always return a FS
write_fs.settext('...')
```

Where applicable, I added `FIXME` and `TODO` comments, so that this PR does not turn into a 10+ issue fixing mess but only adresses typing.

We discussed this for a while today and figured out the underlying problem. The existing diff behaviour assumes that a new model instance does not have an ID. This seems to be true with, say, sqlite. However, this is not true with postgres. When the "original" instance has the same ID as the "current" instance, the diff will fail to show a change (ins/del) with the related attribute.

I've added a test that will reproduce the bug with the existing Models/Resources in resources_tests.py with sqlite.

If you agree with our analysis, I will need to do one more commit to fix up the existing tests that use `get_diff()`.

Hi, it looks like you're trying to lift a thumb instruction. If you're trying to analyze thumb code you need to use odd addresses, i.e. one greater than the actual value, since that's how valgrind represents thumb mode. see the entry "Why are some ARM addresses off-by-one?" in our [FAQ](https://docs.angr.io/docs/faq.html).

I tracked the issue down! The fix is:

```diff
diff --git a/claripy/frontends/composite_frontend.py b/claripy/frontends/composite_frontend.py
index 2a51a67..ec5b739 100644
--- a/claripy/frontends/composite_frontend.py
+++ b/claripy/frontends/composite_frontend.py
@@ -161,7 +161,7 @@ class CompositeFrontend(ConstrainedFrontend):
             extra_vars = frozenset.union(*(a.variables for a in extra_constraints))
             solvers = [ self._merged_solver_for(extra_vars) ]
             for s in self._solver_list:
-                if len(s.variables | solvers[0].variables) == 0:
+                if s.variables.isdisjoint(solvers[0].variables):
                     solvers.append(s)
         else:
             solvers = self._solver_list
```

I was using `|` instead of `&`, and the result was then cached.

Am 04.02.2016 um 21:34 schrieb Joe Jevnik:
>
> The box type is needed because we use the type of objects in the
> graph. It is sort of unfortunate but luckily it doesn't contribute
> meaningfully to the runtime. Odo is not designed to be used in an
> inner loop situation.
>
> You don't need append from sdmx to another type because you have
> already hooked into the graph by defining the single edge. For
> example, let's walk through this call:
>
> odo('myfile.sdmx', 'postgresl://localhost/db::some_table')
>
> The first thing odo will do is call |resource| on the strings to
> figure out what it is working with. This should give us a |PandasSDMX|
> object and a |sqlalchemy.Table| object respectivly.
>
> Next, odo says, "is this a conversion or an append". It sees that the
> rhs is not a type object but an instance so it knows that this is an
> append operation.
>
> Then it asks, "can we append a |PandasSDMX| into an |sa.Table|. Since
> it cannot do this directly it looks to find a path through the graph.
> Luckily we have already defined an edge from |PandasSDMX| to
> |pd.DataFrame| and odo comes with an edge from |pd.DataFrame| to
> |sa.Table|.
>
> We then perform the needed conversions so that we can append the data.
>
> |discover| should return a datashape object, not a string. This can be
> parsed with |datashape.dshape|. I am not sure how to get the shape
> information from one of the response objects you have showed but I
> imagine there is some way. If not, just convert to a dataframe and
> then discover the dataframe.
>
> You might be interested in a notebook I did for a short odo tutorial
> at strata:
> https://github.com/cpcloud/strata-nyc-2015/blob/master/Extending%20Odo.ipynb.
> There is no prose as it was live demo; but, I think it will help show
> an end to end implementation for a trivial type. I will try to pair up
> some examples and prose from this discussion and turn it into docs for
> odo.
>
> —

> <https://github.com/blaze/odo/issues/412#issuecomment-180034872>.

> 关键是在调用者那边是如何用的。这些内容在调用者那边基本都是对象。如果不是返回对象，那调用者不得不封装这个json。另外，在我看来，json 是数据格式，不是实体，而是实体的一种表现。json 的作用在于存储，但缺少操作的能力。或许我们可以搞一些 serializer 去输出不同的格式，或者在对象上提供输出这些格式的方法。

如果只是用来做简单的公众号相关的功能，很多东西都不需要存储，调用之后直接从 dict 中取数据最简便、最少 overhead。Python is not Java，不希望变成和那些 Java 的微信 SDK 一样封装一切。我希望保持核心简单，然后可以做一些扩展。

I made an attempt at providing a `padding` option.  If it is set to `None`, an exception is raised if tight packing is not possible.  Otherwise, `np.full` is used to initialize the array to the value of `padding`.  One potential usage might be to set the padded regions to `np.nan` instead of zeros.

The primary change is the addition of a `_determine_coeff_array_shape` utility function to predetermine the output size and whether tight packing is possible:

```python
    # determine size of output and if tight packing is possible
    arr_shape, is_tight_packing = _determine_coeff_array_shape(coeffs)

    # preallocate output array
    if padding is None:
        if not is_tight_packing:
            raise ValueError("array coefficients cannot be tightly packed")
        coeff_arr = np.empty(arr_shape, dtype=a_coeffs.dtype)
    else:
        coeff_arr = np.full(arr_shape, padding, dtype=a_coeffs.dtype)
```

After almost two years we finally get to close this issue. This was fixed / added over a series of recent commits.

Some details:

The data sinks now support expiration timeouts! I.e. you can specify how long you want data to "live" in the cache or diskstore. If a piece of data is accessed that has expired, it will be removed from the data sink and the data will be refreshed. Currently, data will not be removed from the data sink until it is accessed, and it's up to you as the user to remove "old" data if it's taking up too much space / memory. You can do this via one of the following two methods:  The settings (available via `cass.configuration.settings`) now has 1) `.clear_sinks` and 2) `.expire_sinks` which 1) remove things from all data sinks and 2) remove all expired data from all data sinks. You can specify how long you want data to live in a datasink in your settings.

Hi @NikolausDemmel!
I'll try to provide the information that I have, and will do my best to get the auto-exposure algorithm author to comment. 

So, regarding item 2:
It is surprisingly tricky to provide custom values with each frame of a UVC device. With UVC 1.5 spec, the closest thing to "standard" is per-frame metadata. This feature was introduced in Windows 10 (you can get some info [here](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-hardware/drivers/stream/uvc-extensions-1-5) and for Linux, Intel is actively involved in getting this functionality [up-streamed](https://patchwork.linuxtv.org/patch/38581/)) That said, the alternatives are to embed data into pixels (F200) or expand the frame by a couple of lines (R200). 
ZR300 is embedding 4 bit frame number into the least significant bits of the first pixels to minimize visual artifacts and 8 bit (if I recall correctly) actual exposure. The resulting exposure has one bit less then the corresponding control, due to processing limitations in firmware. 

Regarding item 3:
I believe exposure and gain take affect after two frames. In addition, you should take into account USB and V4L subsystems latency. Usual latency of fish-eye stream is about 15 milliseconds, but if the processing is slow and the host is busy, we allocate up to 4 extra frames for V4L2 to use. This can cause extra latency. 

There are most likely ways to optimize the convergence rate (we experimented with a few) but overall the algorithm was designed with a specific usage in mind: motion tracking with SLAM. For this task, gradual convergence is often better because it makes it easier to track features in the image. May I ask what is the use case you have in mind?

If anyone comes across this, it's because directly changing the texture in the material (binding the TextureEvent  to depthMat.Material.maintexture, as in the examples) changes the shared material, not the copy of it made for the raw image renderer. To get around this, change the texture in the RawImage (bind the TextureEvent to DepthImage.RawImage.texture).

It might be helpful to change the examples to do this, or put it in the docs somewhere. It will also come in handy if someone has multiple cameras and wants to use the same material for all of them.

@geigerj Good points, all. :-) I am not sure I am convinced. I need to figure out the right thing to say to GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python#3083, so let's figure out what that is. My best guess is that my change might not be where we end up, but the status quo is probably wrong also.

> Simple users of the GAPIC should need to know that the gRPC layer exists. Therefore, they should never encounter gRPC errors directly. They should be able to assert that if an RPC error occurs while calling a GAPIC method, it will be a GaxError.

My gut tells me that this requirement is correct, at least as a design principle. This also has the advantage that if you want to, you can `except GaxError` and expect to get most/all things.

I am not as sure that it is correct in "unknown" cases (which is what this is). So, the fundamental exception here is something that our code was not ready for and literally caught with `except Exception`. So, the question is, which is better, to send up a "wrapped" version of the exception (with a not-obvious API), or send up the unrecognized exception to the caller?

I could see a pretty strong case that if we get GRPC errors that we should be able to handle and the code is unrecognized, we treat those as bugs that need to be wrapped in a `GaxError` and given an appropriate/helpful error.

Bottom line: I agree with you that we should avoid sending up transport-specific errors as a general practice. But, I am less sure that handling unknown cases with a sword this broad is wise.

I am intentionally inviting debate / disagreement here. Tell me I am wrong. :-)

> (Meanwhile, advanced users can still access the underlying gRPC error via the attributes of the GaxError.)

Do we document how to do this?

> One should be able to configure any transport layer of GAX without changing its behavior. For example, if we implemented GAX support for HTTP/JSON, a RPC error that occurs during retrying should _still_ be a GaxError.

Again, this sounds right as a design principle; I am unsure it should apply to unknown cases.

> The impact of both of these is that gRPC errors are *never* raised from GAX, since ApiCallables are *always* wrapped to ensure they [always raise GaxErrors when an exception occurs](https://github.com/googleapis/gax-python/blob/master/google/gax/api_callable.py#L355). It is inconsistent only to return the underlying error in the retry case.

I think you are wrong here. As best as I can read this code, `to_catch` is a whitelist. It will not catch any old error; it will only catch the errors it knows how to deal with. The proposed change would make retry errors act _more_ like this code, would it not?

I think we can kill 2 birds...

* Tests are inadequate, inefficient, and split up.
* certbot-auto is difficult to reason about and change (see https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/5392#issuecomment-356721814 and some of my preceding comments).

...with one stone.

When I wrote auto_tests.py, certbot-auto behaved pretty much the same on all OSes; the only difference was the selection of bootstrapper. So integration tests were arguably fine and certainly better than nothing. Since then, however, we've accumulated more system-specific branches, leading to a profusion of global variables, often with uncertain semantics and scopes of effect. Also, branchy functions like `DeterminePythonVersion()` cry out for unit tests, both for speed and for pointing more precisely to failures.

Let's make certbot-auto itself more modular—that is, looser-coupled. Let's tighten those variable scopes and rigorously define the behavior of individual functions, then unit-test the ones that have a lot of branches. This will also make the whole mess easier to change. I'm not saying it will be easy, given the limitations of the Bourne shell, but I bet we can figure something out. We may even be able to move the latter phases into a different language. I'm happy to help sketch up solutions.

Just had the same issue, that is because ``pip install -U`` did overwrite the python modules installed from the RPM.

To solve this I did the following:

### issue

```
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/urllib3/contrib/pyopenssl.py", line 147, in _validate_dependencies_met
    raise ImportError("'pyOpenSSL' module missing required functionality. "
ImportError: 'pyOpenSSL' module missing required functionality. Try upgrading to v0.14 or newer.
```

### solution
```
[root@kolla ~]# rpm -qf /usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/OpenSSL/
pyOpenSSL-0.13.1-3.el7.x86_64
[root@kolla ~]# rpm -qf /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/urllib3
python-urllib3-1.10.2-2.el7_1.noarch

[root@kolla ~]# rpm --nodeps -e python-urllib3
[root@kolla ~]# rm -rf /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/urllib3/
[root@kolla ~]# yum -y install python-urllib3
```

### issue
```
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/requests/__init__.py", line 90, in <module>
    from urllib3.exceptions import DependencyWarning
ImportError: cannot import name DependencyWarning
[root@kolla ~]# rpm -qf /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/requests
python-requests-2.6.0-1.el7_1.noarch
```

###  solution
```
[root@kolla ~]# rpm --nodeps -e python-requests
[root@kolla ~]# rm -rf /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/requests
[root@kolla ~]# yum -y install python-requests   
```

Hi @victor-lund,

I think this is a problem with your Certbot version. According to our changelog, Certbot's support for renewal hook directories was only added in version 0.19.0. So this functionality isn't yet present in your version of Certbot.

There are various ways that you can get a newer version of Certbot if you'd like, but you'll probably have to go outside of your OS package repositories in order to do so.

I was going to suggest that you could emulate this behavior by making a single script that calls `run-parts` on the appropriate directory and then calling that script as a hook, but even `--deploy-hook` was only added in 0.17 (the corresponding hook would have been called `--renew-hook` in 0.10.2).

By the way, `certbot renew` is always noninteractive, so you don't have to specify `--noninteractive` to make it so.

@MerlinSmiles , @peendebak we also use the date_time format as an implicit unique ID. However we sometimes run into the problem that there are multiple files with the same date-timestamp (some experiments generate multiple datasets per second). I guess the uuid solves this by going to a finer resolution. 

Another idea (that can be incorporated with the above) would be to use a certain aspect of git-hashes. The git hashes are generally long. However if you provide enough characters (~7) to be unique the software will automatically select the right hash. We could implement the same functionality for the qcodes hashes so that if you specify yymmddhhmm (and no seconds) it will still find it because there are no other datasets corresponding to it. 

tl;dr
* timestamp based hashes 👍 
* Allow reference by partial hash (like git hash)

This PR decreases performance for

```python
%time l = ray.get([f.remote() for _ in range(10000)])
```

from about 1.3s to about 1.6s.

I think a natural solution to this is to do more batching (in particular to flush from workers on a timer instead of after every task). However, that runs into some of the thread-safety issues brought up in #2248, so I may put that on hold for now.

I would say that it might be better to just reuse some existing process supervisor here instead of reimplementing all this and figuring our all the details on how to manage subprocesses on various systems.

Log output is always written to stderr because stream data can be written to stdout by using the `-O` parameter. If the info and warn log channels would write to stdout instead, they would need to write to stderr once this parameter gets used, which would be confusing and inconsistent.

See here:
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/0.5.0/src/streamlink_cli/main.py#L609-L612

If you want log output to appear in stdout, you can simply redirect the pipes like this in bash:
```bash
streamlink --loglevel debug URL QUALITY 2>&1
```

Btw, ffmpeg does the same and always logs to stderr, too:
https://ffmpeg.org/ffmpeg.html

> By default the program logs to stderr.

@WtfJoke I see you are using windows. You should be calling pdf2text.py with a 'python' before. e.g.:
`python PATH_TO_PYTHON_SCRIPTS\pdf2text.py`
By calling pdf2text.py directly windows will try to open it with the configured default program, which might be python 2.7 or something else you may have configured before.
But even if you configure it to be opened by python3 it will not pass the args forward if you call it directly, so always call a "*.py" script with "python" preceding it in windows.

From the discord.py documentation:

```py
reverse: bool
        If set to true, return messages in oldest->newest order. Recommended
        when using with "after" queries with limit over 100, otherwise messages
        will be out of order.
```
This also (potentially) explains the previously inconsistent behavior (though I'm not sure). We couldn't use `reverse=True` with the previous loop strategy and an early break, and `reverse=False` breaking on a message date isn't safe due to this either.

So basically, if I want to make an async web framework, I had the choice between asyncore, tornado, twisted and gevent. All of them incompatible. Then came asyncio. Now I have curio and trio. And if I choose wrong I'm stuck with it, on a little island of incompatible IO API. After all, you can create a lib to like h2 to be independent from IO, but not a whole framework.

I like diversity in FOSS, but JS and C# have imperfect solutions running fine so they can concentrate on building stuff on top of it.

I feel it's like the DE in linux. On windows and mac, there is one imperfect DE and people just build apps for them and don't bother. In linux, you have many, many choices. And as a results, building anything with custom windows decoration, system tray, deep DE workflow integration, etc. rarely happens because you have too many targets. People have said many time "then just build for one" or "having many choices don't remove your freedom" and the like but in practice, anybody building an app on linux knows the pain. Even targetting the most popular solution just became a loosing move since Ubuntu dropped unity.

I'm really afraid that we will see the same happening with async on Python. From not enough good solutions to too many solutions so eventually no essential high level tooling will exist.

It's not a rant against your work, I think your concept is very interesting. But I wonder if it's not just giving in to the XKCD standard syndrom.

That would be the point though :-). (No pun intended.) When you type `open_splitpoint`, you'd be marking a place in the call stack where you could potentially hang off other children/sprouts/whatever:

```
f1 -> f2 -> (splitpoint) -> f3

This doesn't actually do anything. Then later, when you call `start_soon`, that's when something actually happens:

```
f1 -> f2 -> (splitpoint) -> f3

> Basically the way it's written, if a background task in a fixture crashes, then it causes the actual test to raise Cancelled, and then ... we absorb that exception. 

I guess a less violent way of achieving this would be to wrap the `yield` of the fixture  with a `try/finally` block to have the teardown part executed no matter what.

> So I'm still wondering if it might not end up being simpler all around to stick with a nursery_fixture fixture that all the fixtures are supposed to use.

The trouble I see with the nursery fixture is you don't have a simple way cancel the coroutine you submitted:

```python
@pytest.fixture
async def my_fixture():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        listeners = await nursery.start(await trio.serve_tcp, handle_client, 0)
        yield listeners
        nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
```

becomes:

```python
@pytest.fixture
async def my_fixture(nursery):
    with trio.open_cancel_scope() as cancel_scope:
        listeners = await nursery.start(await trio.serve_tcp, handle_client, 0)
        yield listeners
        cancel_scope.cancel()
```

So in the end we didn't get any benefit from the nursery fixture. Worst, having to handle our own cancel scope is something this not as usual as directly using a nursery so I guess it makes things more complicated.
Obviously we could instead automatically call the `nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()` once the test is over. But this would break the `explicit is better than implicit` zen of Python, so not sure it is worth it.
Yeah, the problem is that in general async generator fixtures could appear anywhere in the fixture DAG, so we whatever we do with them is going to need to somehow involve a recursion over that DAG.

import io

from torch import nn
import torch.utils.model_zoo as model_zoo
import torch.onnx

# Super Resolution model definition in PyTorch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.init as init


class SuperResolutionNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, upscale_factor, inplace=False):
        super(SuperResolutionNet, self).__init__()

        self.relu = nn.ReLU(inplace=inplace)
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(1, 64, (5, 5), (1, 1), (2, 2))
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(64, 64, (3, 3), (1, 1), (1, 1))
        self.conv3 = nn.Conv2d(64, 32, (3, 3), (1, 1), (1, 1))
        self.conv4 = nn.Conv2d(32, upscale_factor ** 2, (3, 3), (1, 1), (1, 1))
        self.pixel_shuffle = nn.PixelShuffle(upscale_factor)

        self._initialize_weights()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.relu(self.conv1(x))
        x = self.relu(self.conv2(x))
        x = self.relu(self.conv3(x))
        x = self.pixel_shuffle(self.conv4(x))
        return x

    def _initialize_weights(self):
        init.orthogonal_(self.conv1.weight, init.calculate_gain('relu'))
        init.orthogonal_(self.conv2.weight, init.calculate_gain('relu'))
        init.orthogonal_(self.conv3.weight, init.calculate_gain('relu'))
        init.orthogonal_(self.conv4.weight)

# Create the super-resolution model by using the above model definition.
torch_model = SuperResolutionNet(upscale_factor=3)

# Load pretrained model weights
model_url = 'https://s3.amazonaws.com/pytorch/test_data/export/superres_epoch100-44c6958e.pth'
batch_size = 1    # just a random number

# Initialize model with the pretrained weights

if torch.cuda.is_available():

# set the train mode to false since we will only run the forward pass.
torch_model.train(False)

# Input to the model
x = torch.randn(batch_size, 1, 224, 224, requires_grad=True)

# Export the model
torch_out = torch.onnx._export(torch_model,             # model being run

                               "super_resolution.onnx", # where to save the model (can be a file or file-like object)
                               export_params=True)      # store the trained parameter weights inside the model file

import onnx
import onnx_caffe2.backend


# Load the ONNX ModelProto object. model is a standard Python protobuf object
model = onnx.load("super_resolution.onnx")

# prepare the caffe2 backend for executing the model this converts the ONNX model into a
# Caffe2 NetDef that can execute it. Other ONNX backends, like one for CNTK will be
# availiable soon.
prepared_backend = onnx_caffe2.backend.prepare(model)

# run the model in Caffe2

# Construct a map from input names to Tensor data.
# The graph of the model itself contains inputs for all weight parameters, after the input image.
# Since the weights are already embedded, we just need to pass the input image.
# Set the first input.
W = {model.graph.input[0].name: x.data.numpy()}

# Run the Caffe2 net:
c2_out = prepared_backend.run(W)[0]

# Verify the numerical correctness upto 3 decimal places

This is actually already possible (although it is not well documented). Class `sentinelhub.constants.AwsConstants` contains all filenames used for download. Therefore you can download only metafiles with:

```Python
from sentinelhub import AwsTileRequest, AwsConstants

request = AwsTileRequest(tile=tile_name, time=time, aws_index=aws_index,
    bands=[],
    metafiles=AwsConstants.S2_L1C_METAFILES, 
    data_folder=data_folder,
    data_source=DataSource.SENTINEL2_L1C)

request.save_data()
```

By the way, if you download data in same structure as available at AWS we figured the best default option is to download all bands and no metadata. That is because in most use cases only bands are needed. However if you download into .SAFE format (specified with `safe_format=True`) the default option is to download all metadata as well (except for `tileInfo.json` and `productInfo.json` which are not part of the .SAFE structure).

I don't understand what you mean by "the scripts are located in bin itself." Generally they are located in `{sys.prefix}/bin`. The exact location depends on whether its an OS-package-manager-installed Python (in which case it's frequently `/usr/bin`) or a Python you compiled yourself (in which case it's `/usr/local/bin` by default, but could be anything at all if you used the `--prefix` compile-time option.) Out of all of those cases, a Python you compiled yourself, with a manually-specified prefix, is the only case where it's likely that the Python's bin directory is not already on your shell PATH by default.

Regardless, I don't see any reason for virtualenv to add any path outside of the virtualenv to your shell PATH. The system python is not part of the virtualenv. If you haven't already configured your shell to have access to a given Python's bin directory, why should virtualenv presume that you want it there?

`bin/activate` does add to the shell PATH, yes, but it does not remove anything from it. So, as I said, if you want a given Python's bin directory (or any other directory) on the PATH, add it, and virtualenv won't touch it. In the common cases, it's generally already there.

For the record, all I did was put in other people's fixes into the latest version and test :-)
